I have found that each course has different structures and settings and i am not sure if there has been a role out of powerpoints and slides that the teachers should be using?.

label: Complaint
comment: The legal subjects are the best in this degree. It provides context and the reasons as to why the criminal justice system is the way it is. There needs to be more focus on other groups that touch the criminal justice system. There is far too much emphasis on First Nations people. Whilst there is justification for the inclusion of this group, they are not the only group. Basically I feel this is degree is an decolonising and First Nations people degree. I have spoken with many other students who all feel the same. The reality is that the criminal justice system touches many groups and all must be considered. This degree needs to be more diverse and inclusive. There are also many irrelevant subjects that were not clear where the topic was going. Also the constant 'reflecting' needs to be limited to one subject. It's repeated over and over in other subjects where students want to learn the material, no consistently 'reflect' on it. Critically analyse, yes, but not this constant circular 'reflecting'.

label: Complaint
comment: My exchange. Funding for the exchange. If a year exchange is compulsory, there should be a budget given to students instead of offering a $7000 loan they need to pay back indexed on hex.

label: Complaint

comment: The facilities and having access to many different studio spaces for practice. I feel that some improvement needs to be made with accessing some spaces. e.g. it is often difficult to gain access to spaces allocated by the resource centres. (e.g. the film development labs and the 2 hour slots - this then is complicated as the space is often very full, making it difficult to produce work).

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: Degree needs to be more streamlined
Need more opportunities to meet people
comment: Degree needs to be more streamlined. Need more opportunities to meet people doing the same degree, as it's very hard to do so on a daily basis.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: More enthusiastic, engaging and knowledgeable lecturers
There needs to be a better support system
There is too much weighing on group tasks
comment: I love that many course and concepts somewhat link together. Also, I like how it practices public speaking.   More enthusiastic, engaging and knowledgeable lecturers/tutors need to be brought in. Instead of implementing compulsory lectures, tackle the issue of absentees by introducing educators who are actually passionate and know what they're teaching. - Many lecturers tend to just waffle to reach the 2 hours mark.- There needs to be a better support system for studies, as PASS classes are usually held when I'm in another INFS class.- There is too much weighing on group tasks for every single course. This means my WAM tanks as a result of freeloaders in my group, which is hard to avoid since we don't know each other when we first start the course.- There is a lack of community in Information Systems. Educators do not facilitate activity in a space where students should be comfortable to speak up on forums and engage with other peers socially.- CoP needs to be removed. Participation marks are immensely subjective and tutors never really record such engagement. The implementation of these marks force students to waste tutorial time on making random statements that every student who has at least attended the lecture or read the slides already knows.Overall, I feel that there is no existing sense of community and willing/natural engagement in and outside the classroom for the Information Systems program. - For reference, Computer Science has a great forum community with students who are passionate for programming, due to enthusiastic professors. They also have many study and employability support systems.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The degree has a wide range of classes you are able to choose from which allows me to explore fields I am more passionate about. Further, due to its flexibility, I am able to choose classes and courses that match with my own schedule and interests. Some aspects that need the most improvement would be the teamwork skills in the course. As most economic courses are focused on more independent work, it would be nice to see a little bit more of team assignments to build our teamwork skills.

label: Complaint

label: Complaint
keywords: did not respond to my emails in time
comment: The program is very involved and challenges students with projects that are closer towards the field of study, giving a very strong foundation to a future career path in the design and architectural industry. Although the workload was heavy and challenging, I enjoyed learning about the discipline. Tutorials are typically quite engaging and tutors often speak to students one on one about their progress and development. From my experience as a student with a neurological disability, staff have all been very understanding and considerate towards my condition and tried to help me in the ways that they could. Even if situations did not go well for me, I could tell that most of them had good intentions. The program in regards to structure and timing is quite inflexible. Often times, lectures and tutorials are only held at one timing per week, meaning students have to reorganise their daily timetables and activities to revolve around uni. This is quite inconvenient for students who have jobs or other appointments during the day, or students who live far from campus and have to commute, as they cannot select alternate class timings to suit them. In addition to this, the overall program is very structured, with lots of courses that require the completion of prerequisite courses. However, sometimes, only specific courses are offered during the term. This means that if students were to slightly differ from the sample program layout, their future progression could be significantly affected. In my case, with my disability, I struggle a lot with the heavy workload and find it hard to get good grades when taking all of the required courses. Yet, the consequences of dropping a single course could stunt my course enrollment for the following terms. Another aspect of the program that affected me is communication with staff. With my condition, I am granted learning adjustments to have more flexibility around task deadlines, but only upon agreement with the course convener. During the year, some of my conveners were very responsive, which really helped me. But some did not respond to my emails in time, or at all, which not only affected my stress levels, but also prevented me from having my extensions implemented and thus impacted my performance and grades in certain tasks.

label: Complaint
keywords: courses make me question their relevance to Food Science

comment: One of the best parts of the Food Science (Honours) program has been discovering the many career pathways it can lead to. The tutors, lecturers, and guest speakers have all been incredibly supportive, encouraging me to see the wide range of opportunities in the food industry. Their guidance has inspired me to think about the different ways I can contribute to the field. Also in my first year, I got to meet so many people outside of the food science course (like chemical engineering and engineering students). Through this, I made close connections with them and made social study groups to help me too. While the program provides valuable skills, there are times when certain courses make me question their relevance to Food Science before starting them. For instance, courses that focus heavily on coding can feel disconnected from the main focus of the degree. It would be helpful if, at the beginning of these courses, there was more explanation on how they are applicable to the field. This way, students could better understand the significance each course brings to their overall education and future careers.

However some courses, such as COMM3501, ECON3210, COMM1190 and COMM1140 were very useful and engaging. I think the entrepreneur courses were good and encouraging students to be innovative and creative and appreciate the inclusion of those in the degree. I think myself and other students I have spoken to agree that commerce courses should teach more skills rather than vague ideas or concepts. I have found the courses I like most are those that teach specific skills (R, financial maths and formulas) as I find these are much more worthwhile and the lessons I've learned are clear. label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: more in-depth practical training on simulation software
more opportunities to work with industry-standard tools and platforms
comment: The best aspects of my Master of Simulation and Immersive Technologies program have been the unique blend of technical and creative challenges, allowing me to explore how immersive environments can transform learning and user engagement. Specifically, the program's focus on combining VR, AR, and MR technologies with real-world applications has greatly expanded my understanding of how these technologies can be used in various industries.The collaborative nature of the coursework, particularly in designing interactive simulations, has enhanced my skills in working with interdisciplinary teams. Additionally, the hands-on projects have helped me develop practical experience, from creating storyboards and educational content to analyzing user engagement in immersive settings. These experiences have not only deepened my technical expertise but also shaped my vision of developing future projects in areas like decentralized games, corporate training, and business simulations.The program's integration of emerging technologies with real-world applications is what makes it stand out and has provided me with the tools and confidence to pursue my entrepreneurial goals in SaaS and beyond. While the Master of Simulation and Immersive Technologies program has provided me with valuable insights, there are a few areas that I believe could be improved. Initially, I expected a stronger focus on technical skills, particularly hands-on experience with immersive technologies like VR, AR, and MR. However, much of the content tends to be more theoretical or broad in nature, sometimes lacking the depth required to fully grasp the technical complexities and tools used in the industry.For instance, I would appreciate more in-depth practical training on simulation software, coding for immersive environments, and technical problem-solving, as these skills are crucial for building effective simulations and immersive experiences. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to have more opportunities to work with industry-standard tools and platforms, as well as to dive deeper into the technical aspects of design and development.Overall, while the conceptual discussions are important, a more focused and technical curriculum would better prepare students for the hands-on challenges in the immersive technology field.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: some demonstrations may not be of a sufficiently advanced level
comment: The curriculum comprises lectures, experiments and workshops, which facilitate effective learning and enable us to consolidate our knowledge on a regular basis. Furthermore, some courses include a weekly quiz, which provides an opportunity to reinforce knowledge gaps. Due to the size of the course, it is not always possible to accommodate all participants effectively. Furthermore, some demonstrations may not be of a sufficiently advanced level to adequately assist students in completing their experiments.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: UNSW can increase its industrial partners
expand its connection to global companies
overview on how real-life working culture is like
comment: Working together in a multicultural and multidisciplinary setting is what I enjoy the most from my experience in UNSW's Master's degree. To provide better Work Integrated Learning, UNSW can increase its industrial partners and expand its connection to global companies - which most students aim to work for. This will provide an overview on how real-life working culture is like and prepare students when entering the job market.

label: Complaint
keywords: management of the research homepages is extremely poor
comment: To engage in cutting-edge research in front-end development at a prestigious international academic institution. The management of the research homepages is extremely poor. Even administrative staff have personal research pages, leading to resource misuse, and every time I file a complaint, no one addresses it. There is very little support for self-funded international students in the humanities, with scholarships and teaching opportunities primarily given to those who already have scholarships. Repeated complaints have gone unanswered. Appeals to the university president and international human rights organizations have led nowhere. I am deeply disappointed with the school's administration. I do not recommend that family members send their children here for a PhD. Very poor experience!I have repeatedly advocated on behalf of the group of self-funded international PhD students in the humanities, calling on the university to create more temporary research assistant positions prioritizing our group for one year after graduation, but have been consistently ignored. Complaints to the ResIT department regarding the misuse of research homepages and public resources have been disregarded, and I have even received retaliatory responses. This is extremely infuriating. We are all human beings, and everyone deserves equal treatment-why are we being treated this way?.

comment: One of the best aspects of the Bachelor of Landscape Architecture (Honours) program has been the way the tutors and course convenors design assignments that directly relate to real-world industry practices. Additionally, working alongside individuals who have industry experience has been invaluable, offering insights and perspectives that enhance my learning experience. One area that could benefit from improvement in the Bachelor of Landscape Architecture (Honours) program is the need for a more structured pipeline guiding students towards real-world industry placement. Having clearer resources that explain how what we learn applies to industry practices would be really valuable. Additionally, more detailed insights into the current job market and practical guidance on how to enter the industry would help bridge the gap between academic learning and professional development.

label: Complaint
keywords: courses can seem like they lack purpose
comment: The best aspects of my arts program have been the manner in which all the content interconnects and can be utilised in courses interchangeably. The engagement of teaching staff has also been vital for stimulating my learning and engagement in the program, this has allowed me to receive detailed feedback and has resulted in assessments being far more rewarding and personal to me. Often the courses can seem like they lack purpose or development. For example I have had multiple instances of completing one course and then the following term learning the same content but in a more basic form which does not benefit my learning at all and results in the course feeling like a revisiting of prior content rather than a broadening of knowledge and learning. I have also found that some courses have little direction, often flip flopping between too many concepts for any to really be covered in any detail or to benefit my understanding.

label: Complaint
keywords: Many lecturers are neither good at communication
comment: My degree is broad in that there are many different schools of thought, academic disciplines as well as a diverse set of content of which we are made aware. So far, I have had the best experience with philosophy units. The teachers in these are often more informed, rigorous, and devoted to student-learning and the pursuit of knowledge. 1) Teaching. Many lecturers are neither good at communication in lectures nor feedback on assignments.They often lack clarity in exactly what propositions they, or a viewpoint they are presenting, affirm to be true or false. Moreover, my lecturers often fail to be concise. This is important for helping students stay engaged, model good communication skills (lecturers often desire concise writing in essays - written communication), and to avoid wasting time. Given that we only have 9 teaching weeks, with few contact hours in each (see below), this last point is especially pertinent. However, in written feedback lecturers tend to be too concise. They often fail to provide insight into how I can improve my grade and communication skills in general. Markers also often don't take time to engage substantively with the arguments I present which disheartening and creates the impression that university is about getting grades, not knowledge. My lecturers also often fail to be engaging. It is clear that my lecturers are primarily researchers, NOT teachers. It is highly disappointing that university ranking is tied to research output and skews incentives away from the passing on and dissemination of knowledge and critical thinking - an essential function of the university - to (increasingly specialised) knowledge production within an increasingly alienated elite. PLEASE CHANGE THIS.  2) Contact hours. I only receive 3-4 contact hours per unit per week. Contact hours, I believe, have the potential to be one of the more intellectually stimulating and otherwise beneficial ways of digesting course content and spurring critical thinking (for example, because of the instant feedback and correction a lecturer is able to provide to students, or the relative ease with which the lecturer can pass on a passion for learning, and thus inspire future knowledge production). However, it is difficult to do so in 3-4hrs. Given that my lecturers tend to be poor communicators (see above), perhaps it is a good thing that we don't have more contact hours. However, tutorials (particularly in philosophy units) tend to be fairly productive and engaging, but unfortunately almost exclusively last for only 1hr. Please lengthen tutorial times to 1.5-2hrs, while keeping lecture times at least as long.

label: Safe
comment: The best thing about Bachelor of Social Sciences is the versatility that it brings in my learning. The reason why I chose this degree over commerce is because it allows me to immerse myself in the dynamics of International Business and Political Sciences within the scope of policy making. From prior knowledge and learnings from those around me, I realised policy is a fundamental in life that can both control, dictate and support our everyday lives. I realised it is important to understand it from a researcher's perspective to compare and contrast with a civilian's perspective. Furthermore, with my prior background in economics, psychology and theory of knowledge from the IB curriculum, it had allowed me to further expand my horizons on the aspects of life that policy making can influence which include sociocultural, psychological wellbeing, physical, economical and political. I would say while the course in itself is self explanatory for me and is well taught for the most part, I believe it would be better if there was more opportunities for extensive knowledge about policy making in the earlier courses. The earlier courses operate too much on given assumptions and tend to misguide to the idea that as a policy maker, we should look through an idealistic lens instead of a realistic one to compare.

label: Safe
comment: Competent and approachable lecturers, good content and course structure, relevant assessments. Class sizes should be small. I really like that Law at UNSW is taught in seminars with fewer people, but the amount of students in these seminars has gradually been increasing, reducing the value of having seminars in the first place. I have found that if my course involves seminars with 40 participants or less, it is much easier to be engaged with the lecturer, collaborate with peers, and engage in constructive class discussions. Being able to do these things has been incredibly valuable. I also think that reading materials should continuously be reviewed and updated because some courses still use bad textbooks with dense, verbose language, poor explanations of concepts, while lacking adequate explanation important principles. I also think law schools should emphasise the public service aspect of being a lawyer and being legally trained, rather than always promoting careers in large corporate law firms.

The classrooms. The classes tend to have a substantial number of students, and the classrooms are either too hot or too cold. Other classrooms need a better design to make it easier to see the lecturer - for instance, some classrooms have lots of tables and this means half the students on each table will be facing the opposite way to the professor. label: Complaint
keywords: back to face to face learning
comment: The best aspects of the Masters Degree has been the fact that we are back to face to face learning. It is much more engaging than online coursework as it encourages focus, stimulates discussion and creates a routine that helps with completing coursework in a timely manner. Most of the tutors and course convenors have made a good effort at showing their concern for students learning and development. Although most course convenors demonstrate their concern for students' learning and development it often stops at course work and course content. This year in particular I do believe that the course content has been aimed at teaching us more 'real world' applications of the industry which has been great. However, what I believe could and should be improved is the structure and expected deliverables for our courses each semester. Currently with the trimester structure there are 3 assessments per course. I have had 3 courses each semester meaning that each semester I have at least 9 assessments to complete. In some cases some courses have two parts to an assignment with a sizeable amount of work for each part. An example of this was semester 2, where we had 3 subjects, 3 assessments for each with the exception of 1 subject wherein for 2 of the assessments it had two parts to the assignment meaning that this course alone had 5 assessments on top of the other two courses that we had bringing the total number of assessments to 11 assessments to complete in a semester of 10 weeks. This paired with the assessment schedule which had assessment clashes and different assessments for different subjects placed one after the other made it a very difficult semester which created a lot of pressure inside and outside of uni. I strongly believe that the best solution to this problem which occurs almost every semester is to change the structure from 3 assessments per course to 2. This would create a more positive work environment for students as well as tutors and convenors and would allow for students to produce a higher resolution of work as they would have more time to complete the work.

label: Complaint
keywords: smaller methods classes were incredibly helpful
comment: The smaller methods (specialisation) classes were incredibly helpful. They felt like a community of professionals working together to share resources and hone ideas. Sadly some coursework has been very outdated. Hopefully after raising it at the time it has since been improved. Often subjects felt thin in content, often shallowly focusing on a small idea rather than developing depth or breadth of subject. Services outside of the courses were often difficult to contact and get a hold of and seemed ineffective when you were able to contact them.

label: Safe

label: Safe
comment: The best aspects of my Doctorate of Electrical Engineering have been the opportunity to work on cutting-edge projects in nanophotonic, where I've been able to push the boundaries of knowledge, particularly in composite semiconductors, meta surface design, and their applications. The program's focus on hands-on research has allowed me to collaborate with world-class researchers, deepen my understanding of advanced simulation tools like FDTD, RCWA, PlanOpSim, and programming, and contribute to novel designs such as strong coupling, efficient meta lenses, and holographic imaging systems. Additionally, the intellectual freedom to explore various subfields, along with the support from UNSW and access to state-of-the-art resources, has been incredibly rewarding. While the Doctorate of Electrical Engineering - Research program has provided me with an enriching experience, there are areas that could benefit from improvement. One aspect is the need for more structured opportunities for interdisciplinary collaboration, and enhancing access to cleanroom device fabrication would further accelerate research progress.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: One of the best aspects of pursuing a Master of Engineering (Biomedical) at UNSW has been the opportunity to engage with cutting-edge research and technology. The curriculum is well-structured, with a strong focus on practical, hands-on learning through labs and projects, allowing me to apply theoretical knowledge to real-world problems. The faculty, particularly experts like Dr. [specified name removed] [specified name removed] [specified name removed] [specified name removed] [specified name removed] [specified name removed] and [specified name removed] [specified name removed] have been incredibly supportive and knowledgeable, providing a great depth of understanding in areas such as cellular and tissue engineering, neuromodulation, and bionics. The diversity of students in the program also adds to the learning experience, fostering collaboration and the exchange of ideas. The interdisciplinary approach within biomedical engineering, combining aspects of mechanical, electrical, and biological sciences, has opened up many avenues for exploration and specialization. However, one area where UNSW could improve is in providing better resources to help students land jobs. While the academic experience has been stellar, more robust career support, networking opportunities, and industry connections would greatly benefit students as they transition into the workforce. label: Complaint
comment: The best aspect of my Masters Degree is the event held by UNSW business school. Business school is trying their best to help students to build employability. These events include networking events, compliance events, self-exploration workshops, and so on. These events are held weekly and contain wonderful knowledge and diversity. People I meet at business school are all friendly and passionate about helping others. I feel a great sense of belonging to UNSW Business School. The quality of courses at UNSW Business School has collapsed obviously, especially in some popular courses. Firstly, the quality of some tutors even lectors has decreased. Industry-experienced teachers are now fewer and a lot of PhD students without professional training or any industry experience replaced them. A lot of teachers, especially tutors just read the slides drably with less interesting examples or explanations. One of my tutors even never knew what is double pass when she was asked the formula of the final exam, even this rule was written on the slides.

label: Complaint
comment: Very few. UNSW as a whole needs to abandon the trimester system as it requires too much time and commitment from the student. This system hinders the ability for international students to find work and explore social interests, both negating the student's opportunity to develop work-relevant learnings and understanding the local way-of-life which is crucial to the specific learnings of the degree. The system itself is also inefficient because the individual semester is too short for project development, and this places pressure on the tutors to give concessions to student (i.e. extensions, reduced deliverables) which also reduces learning outcomes. It is also inefficient as in the same 2-year period, I could go to other universities which offer more time for a project which leads to a more developed project and more learning outcome, less time to study while doing more meaningful work while getting the same credits, and less mental stress.

comment: Conducting research on my own. Collaboration with others beyond your supervisor. Structure of the program (3.5 years are just too short and cannot compete with PhD graduates from other countries). Most importantly, the school or more broadly Australian doctoral programs need to have more thorough selection criteria (e.g., GRE scores). This is necessary to keep the quality of students. Some are just considering starting PhD as an interim option when they cant get a job and this in result, causes a huge negative impact on the uni's resources and time. There were at least three students who quit during my candidature (and these students are mostly chosen for their work experience in the first place, which is not so useful in academia!!! Never seen them making any meaningful academic contributions during their PhD since they're old and have family obligations/responsibilities that impedes their study).

It would be great if I could get an internship at an ISP so that I could strengthen my research and apply it to ISP networks.

comment: I have a fantastic primary supervisor who provides excellent support throughout my PhD journey. The research centre itself is a positive and supportive space, fostering a sense of safety and belonging. There's healthy competition, but nothing overwhelming, which makes it a great place to grow both personally and professionally. The PhD program is heavily focused on enhancing academic skills, such as writing articles and delivering presentations. However, it would be even more beneficial if there were greater support for developing skills relevant to careers outside of academia.

label: Constructive Feedback

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The best aspects include access to cutting-edge resources, collaboration with expert supervisors, and the opportunity to engage in impactful, interdisciplinary research. The supportive research environment and access to high-quality academic networks have significantly enriched my learning and professional growth. I believe the program could benefit from more student engagement activities and the inclusion of talks by talented speakers from both research and industry. Additionally, some HDR students with families face challenges due to the lack of support services, such as childcare. Other universities, like ANU, offer such support, and implementing similar initiatives at ADFA would greatly assist students with families in managing their studies.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Engaging with my supervisors during our monthly catch up meetings and receiving their feedback on my written work. First year HDRs should have some mandatory research methods modules to take.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: integration into the wider student community
comment: The program involves a lot of independent study, be it reading or learning how to do things on your own. The instruction on laboratory techniques in our laboratory is very good. During my program, I have learned how to work with animals and how to handle and process tissue. Integration into the wider student community. I am doing my program off campus at an associated research institute. Even though we are very close to the Kensington campus, all events that would facilitate socialising with other students are held during work times, where it is somethings not possible to attend these events due to experiments or deadlines. This has led to me not knowing where anything is located on campus and complete unfamiliarity with some University structures.

label: Complaint
keywords: time that I have to do research is very limited
comment: The flexibility and independence of conducting research is probably the best aspect of the program. Furthermore, the good relationship with my supervisors help foster collaboration and productivity. As a PhD student, sometimes I need to apply for data that requires contracts, I think the speed of reply from the Research Grant & Contracts team need to be improved. It took me 3 months to receive a simple confirmation of "Yes" and the problem is still not resolved at the time of writing this. As a student, the time that I have to do research is very limited and this hinders my ability to follow the set timeline.

label: Constructive Feedback

I'm learning a lot of new terms related to mining, which is great.The workplace is good, although it feels like there could be a bit more diversity since many of my colleagues are from China. But everyone is really friendly. In my PhD, I need to do a lot of fieldwork, including visiting mine sites. However, getting access to these sites is extremely difficult. It would be much better to have a clear framework or strong connections with the industry and mining school to make these arrangements easier. This would save time and reduce the hassle, allowing for more efficient research progress.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: resources are disposable to all educators
comment: I have enjoyed being challenged by both the volume and standard of content being covered and studied by myself and all other students. I have also enjoyed the structure of the course and the way it involves students to study and practice in a similar manner to what will be found in the work place of our career. As a proud Aboriginal man and descendant of a Stolen Child, I find the First Nation's content to at times seem to only be included "for the sake of it". Also, I have found that in Scenario Groups and smaller group settings, I am looked upon for answers and information by students and sometimes teachers, demonstrators, lecturers etc. Whilst intentions may be genuine, this field slightly out of order as I am at the University to primarily learn and not teach. Yes, I may have the option to not say anything but I feel the need for correction and education when certain areas of my culture are mis-interpreted or misunderstood as this is fundamentally where divides can originate from, lack of information and education. That being said, I would greatly appreciate for the University and universities everywhere to continue to develop their Indigenous programs and ensure that these resources are disposable to all educators and teachers so as to reduce and limit the need for input of students like myself when I should be focusing on my study and development as a medical practitioner as opposed to a source for extra information.

label: Complaint
keywords: not enough spaces for proper installs
comment: I like the Paddington campus. I find the resource centre and the maker space very helpful. Some of the lecturers are fantastic. Some lecturers were not helpful. Tended to be casual staff. The expectation to constantly install work is quite stressful because there are not enough spaces for proper installs. Students are not told that they need to book spaces, not just put a piece of paper on the door, or if a room is empty just install their work there without checking. Staff need to inform students of this.

label: Complaint
comment: Previously it's the right 'and not privilege' to be able to attend learning remotely. It helps with engaging in the course that fits into being able to work. The ability to work remotely and expectation to do placement where I have to work at an organisation where I don't get paid and am forced to quit my job or and find work on the weekends to make ends meet. It's pretty disgusting. Why should I do 2 placements for 3 months and not get paid for work I am doing that is basically the same as the staff there. This is wage theft, or modern day slavery or engaging in unfair and unreasonable expectations for people to be forced into. Either pay students for the work they are doing or remove working for free. It should be against the law.

label: Complaint

comment: The support of the faculty wellbeing officer. There need to be more wellbeing officers. When bullying and sexual harassment is brought up, students are encouraged to stay silent as this is the prevalent culture in medicine.

label: Constructive Feedback

comment: Some very good lecturers and interesting content (e.g. operating systems).I liked the focus on fundamentals and understanding computers in first year (e.g. learning C, taking some assembly).Courses like comp2041 are interesting and very applicable to working in industry.Course convenors\lecturers do a great job making many resources available to students (lecture recordings/slides, tutorial questions etc.). Depending on the subject, it can either be pretty in depth and rigorous or quite surface level.Group projects can be tedious, especially when you're not doing the class with people you already know. There are some people in group projects who make you wonder how they passed previous courses.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Being able to select the elective courses that I was interested in such as in Humanitarian Engineering, traffic, and sustainability. More integration of the skills and experience required for work in core courses which aren't electives, as they fall short in comparison to elective courses in that regard. Additionally, more support towards helping and guiding students with work-integrated learning as it's a requirement for the program but feels completely separate to the program.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: medical stuff occurring in the background
hard financially to make big artworks
comment: They have encouraged and pushed for me to become more independent and work as an artist confidently with a sense of direction and identity. This is something I have always struggled with. I definitely feel a lot more confident in that but due to my additional needs I would enjoy and get the most out of it by doing another year of that kind of support to be more independent as an artist. I needed more support and encouragement and my professors and tutors were more than happy to provide the support... despite my year of medical stuff occurring in the background. I think in the Final Medium classes e.g drawing 3 and photography 3. Teachers push for students to go bigger. Which yes, technically it would help conceptually but it is so expensive to go bigger and it wasn't in the card for many people which made it difficult for many and was embarrassing at times for many when we got feedback and they would encourage us again. We all understand why and it wasn't meant to be that way but its still hard financially to make big artworks, especially in photography for test prints.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: I have really enjoyed the variety of subjects available and how they also implements different programs and skills that they are recognising are and will become more vital in the workplace in the future (i.e. data analysis, not just analysing the numbers but understand what story it is telling). I believe it would be essential to try and include more practical work in as many subjects as possible. When I was on exchange in ESADE, in more than half of my classes we worked first hand with CEOs and representatives of different companies on projects/ problems that wanted solutions on. So while learning the theory we were building, in groups, proposals and marketing plans on how to resolve these business issues/ building on opportunities. Our work was then presented to these Businesses at the end of the semester where they selected their favourite/ most practical/ realistic proposal and some even implemented it within their marketing strategy. For example, If there is going to be a blood drive at UNSW, one marketing course could get the students to work in groups to make goals, market the drive and implement the theory learnt throughout the course to truly consolidate and ensure students know how to properly transfer the theory learnt in class to the real world. Additionally, it helps students build important networking skills and connections.

I love learning about Psychology and I have been able to do a wide variety of psych classes, not just core subjects, but also elective areas of interest. Uni culture, I feel like it's been so different since COVID.

label: Complaint

label: Complaint
comment: Some of the best aspects this last year have been the guest speakers coming to give lectures or seminars during the course. They are sometimes from industry, sometimes from different branches, and sometimes from related fields, but they've given excellent insight in ways that university academics simply can't (by nature of the academic field). It's also been very nice to see the changes in the course based on the student feedback of previous years. The aspect that most needs improvement is an aspect affecting the whole university life, which is the trimesters. The structure of trimesters means that the course is rushed, the content is briefly covered, and you are constantly completing assessments. There is little time to enjoy the course content because you must constantly think of the next assessment. This means that the entire culture surrounding the engineering faculty is focused on grades, WAM, and "easy HD courses" rather than the actual content we're learning, the courses that sound interesting, and the potential fields we are looking to enter in our futures.

comment: The practical year on third year of the degree. It provides students with the opportunity to gain experience and employment that is relevant to City Planning. label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: practice test materials with worked solutions
support service that provided help with science related topics
comment: The content is very interesting. there's a wide variety in the type of work i do (readings, videos, lectures, etc.) so it never gets too repetitive. practical work is very hands on, and is very relevant to course work. course convener's and demonstrators have consistently been helpful and supportive; it's easy to go up to them if i need help. coursework is structured well; i like how mostly everything is learnt in chronological order. Mid terms and final exams: i would love for more courses to provide more practice test materials with worked solutions so i don't feel like i'm studying blind. i think it would be really beneficial to hold all lectures at a student accessible venue every time. for anatomy, it was hard for me to grasp some aspects as everything was taught purely online and labs were a bit too fast paced. if the lectures were held online, i would have attended to understand certain topics better. i have seen there is a lot of services to help with essays, such as checking them for free or free feedback. it would be awesome if there was a support service that provided help with science related topics, like a question and answer type of thing. i've had to buy chegg multiple times for help on a moodle quiz question because there was no support elsewhere.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The most rewarding aspects of my degree have been the invaluable hands-on experiences, where learning becomes an interactive and immersive process. I truly enjoy engaging with others, collaborating, and being an active participant in the learning journey. The integration of practical components, tutorials, and access to cadaver studies has been particularly enriching, providing me with an in-depth understanding of human anatomy in ways that go beyond textbooks. I am incredibly fortunate to benefit from the world-class resources available to us, both through the dedicated staff at UNSW and the extensive materials provided by the library. What has truly stood out to me is how this degree has deepened my passion for health and exercise. Every tutorial, practical session, and resource has expanded my knowledge, equipping me with the skills and insights I need to succeed in the field of physiotherapy. I am deeply grateful for the countless opportunities that UNSW has offered, from state-of-the-art facilities to academic and professional support. These experiences have solidified my confidence in pursuing my career and have made my educational journey deeply fulfilling. While the degree at UNSW is exceptional in many respects, I believe there is room for improvement in terms of timetable flexibility and class scheduling. One of the challenges I've faced is the difficulty in balancing university commitments with work. With classes scheduled across four days a week, often at times that don't allow for a full day of work, it can be hard to manage both academic responsibilities and the need to support myself financially.The current schedule, with classes sometimes spread out or held at awkward times, leaves little room for meaningful work hours, especially for those who need to sustain themselves or save for the future while studying. Greater flexibility in timetables whether through offering more online classes, consolidating in-person sessions into fewer days, or providing evening or weekend options would make a significant difference. As students, many of us are juggling multiple commitments, including part-time jobs, internships, and extracurricular activities, all of which are essential for both our personal growth and professional development. A more flexible approach would allow us to maintain a healthier balance between study, work, and personal time, making it easier to apply our education to real-world experiences and opportunities without compromising either. I believe that such improvements would not only enhance the overall student experience but also contribute to better mental and financial well-being.

label: Complaint
keywords: workplace has sometimes struggled to respect the agreement with my uni
numpty public servants are permitted to write such uninformed and destructive policies
comment: Flexibility and accommodation of new and unexpected disabilities and medical conditions. Of course they are always professional. But they are also humane, and put people first. I wouldn't change the uni. But there needs to be a clearer understanding in Gov as to what a PhD actually is. I enrolled in a PhD after a detailed written agreement with my Gov employer, allowing flexible leave for the duration of the degree and permission to publish on my topic. This notwithstanding, the workplace has sometimes struggled to respect the agreement with my uni. Those gov employees with no prior experience of HDR think a PhD is just more classes at uni when it's research, public engagement, and publishing. I have a contract in place and it's still been difficult to defend the commitment. Other gov employees who were fellow students have since had to discontinue their PhDs because of employer pressures. This really is unacceptable given how much it costs to fund a PhD student. Gov agencies can't pretend to champion HDR for their employees and then sabotage it this way. There's a long history of this and it must change. That can start with properly researched Dept policies - the one I just reviewed showed that the author had no clue whatsoever about the Dept's own existing policies on research ethics approval, or on the National policy for human research. It beggars belief that such numpty public servants are permitted to write such uninformed and destructive policies.

label: Complaint

broader government decision-making during the pandemic which left many of us financially unsupported
comment: RPR panel is knowledgeable about the subject area and research program. The school runs numerous seminars for people to engage in and I have accessed recordings of tutorials offered by the school and the university when needed. I have become very limited in my in-person and event engagement via the school and university because I have a lot of engagement through my professional roles at a research institute and peak body that is related to my studies. This separation from the university was produced throughout the COVID-19 pandemic which significantly impacted my engagement with the school, university, other students, as well as my research program. The flow-on effects of the pandemic continue to impact the university sector today. This is not the fault of the school or the university but the broader government decision-making during the pandemic which left many of us financially unsupported and unable to progress our research as planned. Greater variation in the timing of school events. Often these are held on the same day at the same time which means for those of us elsewhere employed we are unable to join.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: lack of engagement among data science students is a drawback
comment: The Bachelor of Data Science and Decisions program is structured in a way that gives students a well-rounded perspective. With the option to choose from three specializations, it broadens our career possibilities while still allowing us to focus on a specific area, which I find incredibly useful. However, I do feel that the lack of engagement among data science students is a drawback, as building a strong community and network is essential in any field. The limited availability of student counseling also affects how well we can navigate academic and career challenges.That said, the skills I've gained so far are ones I can see myself applying in future studies which I can apply in professional settings. One aspect I particularly appreciate is the interdisciplinary nature of the program. The courses are attended by students from various faculties, which allows me to interact with innovative minds from different fields. Whether it's the business school through economics, the engineering school through computer science, or the mathematics and statistics department, these interactions have given me valuable insights into how data science can be applied across multiple disciplines while at the same time, diverse interactions allow students like me to collaborate with innovative minds and explore how data science can be applied across multiple fields. This has opened my eyes to the vast potential of data science and how it can influence everything from business and technology to mathematics and beyond. The Bachelor of Data Science and Decisions program could benefit from improvements in several key areas. First, there is a noticeable lack of student engagement, particularly within the data science cohort. Building a stronger sense of community among students could greatly enhance the learning experience, as collaboration and networking are crucial in this field. Organizing more interactive events, study groups, or workshops could help foster a more engaged and connected student body.Additionally, the availability of student counseling is quite limited. Given the complexity of the coursework and the wide array of career paths available in data science, having more accessible academic and career guidance would be highly beneficial. This would help students better navigate their academic journey and make more informed decisions about their specializations and career trajectories.Lastly, I think the program could integrate more hands-on, real-world projects. While the theoretical knowledge is valuable, more practical experience with real data and industry-related problems would prepare students for the workforce more effectively. This could be achieved through partnerships with industry professionals, internships, or collaborative projects with businesses and organizations.

comment: As a first-year student in the Bachelor of Medical Science program, the best aspects for me have been the introduction to fundamental concepts in medical science and the opportunity to explore different areas within the field. The coursework has been challenging but rewarding, and it has helped me build a strong foundation in subjects like anatomy, physiology, and biochemistry. The supportive learning environment and access to resources, such as labs and experienced instructors, have also been crucial in helping me transition smoothly into university life. Overall, the first year has sparked my interest in the various pathways within medical science and motivated me to pursue further studies in this field. The way some courses are structured. Say for example anatomy we had to memorize labeling then function when I'm sure I would've done better if I studied the function then labeling.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The best aspects of my Diploma in Architecture program have been its emphasis on practical experience, diverse perspectives, and access to advanced facilities. Working on real-world projects has allowed me to apply theoretical knowledge effectively, while collaborating with peers from various backgrounds has enriched my understanding of architecture. The program's cutting-edge technology and tools have facilitated innovative design work, and learning from experienced faculty has provided invaluable insights and guidance. Additionally, the creative freedom encouraged by the program has helped me develop my own design philosophy, and the networking opportunities with industry professionals and alumni have been crucial for building connections and exploring career possibilities. Overall, these elements have made the program a transformative and enriching experience. While my Diploma in Architecture program has been highly valuable, there are areas that could benefit from improvement. Greater emphasis on integrating sustainability principles into the curriculum would better prepare students for the increasing focus on environmental responsibility in architecture. Additionally, enhancing the balance between theoretical coursework and practical experience through more hands-on opportunities and real-world project collaborations could improve students' readiness for professional practice. Expanding the program's focus on emerging technologies, such as advanced design tools and new materials, would help students stay current with industry innovations. Finally, providing more structured career support, including workshops and networking events, could better assist students in transitioning to professional roles. Addressing these areas could further enrich the program and improve its overall impact.

label: Complaint
comment: One of the best aspects of my Masters Degree in Law program has been the support I received from my lecturer, [specified name removed] [specified name removed] Even though I had met the English proficiency requirements for admission, I struggled to understand lectures at the beginning. [specified name removed] assistance was invaluable and encouraged me to continue my studies; without his help, I might have withdrawn from the program. Additionally, I received a lot of support from the school regarding legal matters, which I found very moving. There are several aspects of my Masters Degree in Law program that need improvement. Firstly, some lecturers only allow questions to be asked on the Moodle forum, which feels very restrictive. For example, if I want to confirm whether my ideas align with the final paper topic without making them public, there are no alternative channels for support, which can make students feel isolated in seeking help.Additionally, some professors rely too heavily on group discussions. In these classes, we often have less than five minutes of lecture, followed by group discussions, and then representatives share their group's findings. This format leaves little room for actual learning, as students may not always have accurate information to share.I also hope that the feedback for the final paper goes beyond just a grade. It would be beneficial to receive more detailed feedback on how I can improve my work.Lastly, when seeking help from The Nucleus: Student Hub, I hope students receive more than just generic responses. Sometimes, the answers provided are easily found through a quick Google search. In such cases, it would be more efficient for students to search for the information themselves rather than sending an email. What students really need is practical solutions to their problems, not just a restatement of information from Google or official websites.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Laboratory experiments are usually the highlight, along with assignments/projects that give students freedom, ie a broad question that can be attacked from multiple angles, giving us students different approaches to think critically and solve a problem. For example a chemistry question where we make product B from product A that can be done in a multitude of different ways, and its up to us to figure out how and what way is most efficient and practical to do. Similarly the two classes at the beginning and the end of the degree where the subject is only medicinal chemistry students is great, as it promotes relevant discussions to coursework, future jobs etc, and more small cohort subjects like these within the degree would be great. Most likely the examples that lecturers give, some provide problem questions with no answers which makes them virtually meaningless, that and the lack of examples given when teaching notoriously difficult concepts makes it difficult to grasp from a lecture without outside resources like google, chatgpt, youtube videos, textbooks etc. Along with that, sometimes complex concepts are covered at too quick of a pace, where in an exam setting our knowledge of said concept is expected to be much higher than what we could have possibly learned when its covered in a short time ie 5 min in a lecture to demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of a difficult concept in an exam worth a lot of weighting.

label: Complaint
comment: The course content is hard but interesting, the labs are quite good and the tutors are generally helpful with problems you are stuck on. Trimesters really suck, the pacing of the courses compared to a semester system is not good.

label: Safe
comment: The connections you can make in the university. Nothing much it's a standard engineering course you know learn the basics.

label: Complaint
comment: The best aspects have mostly been about how I get to study subject matters that really interest me. Alongside this I have also been able to meet other really talented students and have intriguing conversations about my interests. Additionally, my ELEC1111 and COMP1511 courses were real standouts to me in their quality of structuring and how they conducted assessments in the courses. Mostly in the infrastructure of the CSE server for which I can conduct all my work in one environment instead of a lazy solution by the uni to outsource their marking programs. The areas that need the most improving I believe are the math courses at UNSW. I have not been able to engage with the math courses at this uni well due to how they operate with their lab tests the annoying mobius system. I feel like it just becomes tedious and infuriating to the point that it feels like a chore where I gain no real skills from doing the set activities. This is a real shame to me as I was very intrigued with mathematics in highschool but ever since taking maths at UNSW. I will now avoid any future math courses simply to how UNSW conducts it. I feel as if their new system greatly prioritises the people that don't want to learn maths but would rather pass it. As these online systems especially for the exam really do not encourage actually writing and using the maths learnt in the course and rather more just regurgitating the knowledge. Additionally the exam system is infuriating due to the unexplained errors and the harsh punishments for typos. I would much rather the uni stick to paper exams where there is a real incentive to learn and solve problems as the whole working out can be assessed.

label: Complaint
comment: Subject offering diversity. Industrial experience requirement and thesis are terrible components of the program.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: Assessments and Exams can be improved
comment: Challenging coursework that is stimulating and interesting. Assessments and Exams can be improved. More projects with practical application.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The student community has been very active and enjoyable as well as beneficial to my learning. Feedback and lesson plan clarity, sometimes.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Perhaps the content that had been delivered. Though at times there were inconsistencies, the majority of concepts that were delivered were quite good and did give me some knowledge about the criminal justice system and its impacts in NSW. The same as Social Work, but more practical opportunities in assessment tasks would be extremely helpful in experiencing and witnessing how the criminal justice system operates. In CRIM2020, one of the assessment tasks involved us going to local courts to view cases, and I would like something like that to be replicated in Criminology courses.

label: Constructive Feedback

label: Safe
comment: Overall, the experience of learning again after 10 years in the workforce since completing my bachelors degree has been very enjoyable. Lecturers and course coordinators are doing thier best with the tremendous number of students being loaded on to them. I already hinted previously. There appears to be too many students in the cohort as teachers have all flagged early on that they are under resourced and apologised in advance. Obviously UNSW is eager to enrol as many students as they can in the course even if it means poor learning outcomes for students (especially internationals) who pay huge money to take these courses. I did a course that offered only online tutorials, so we had about 120 students for one tutor, everyone trying to ask questions at the same time. I stopped attending as they were a waste of time. I've also never received any feedback on any written assignment for the course. This has been a major disappointment and totally unexpected after my previous bachelors degree in which all assessments were marked and returned to students with solid feedback. I could go on but to what end? The program is a big UNSW cash cow.

For virtual courses, I would recommend more online sessions For some courses that I did online I would have liked to have more online sessions Not sure how possible it would be, but a 1-1 with each student and the facilitator At least once would be good to help the facilitator understand how the students are going.

comment: The best aspects have been the professional academic staff that have taught me so far in the courses I have completed. I submitted a glowing review of epidemiology last trimester. UNSW creates an environment for online students that is highly conducive to learning and staff reply to very quickly to questions posted. Well done UNSW!. I have only completed two units so far and my experience has been positive. If I could suggest one thing... maybe consider having different units available for the core units of a course. This could help make the course more appropriate to more occupations or disciplines. label: Safe
comment: I love that the program emphasises applying research to real-world problems in education, allowing me to effectively bridge theory and practice. The cohort model provides a really supportive environment and encourages rich discussions and sharing of experiences. My supervisors have been fantastic - very, very supportive. Certain aspects needed more guidance, particularly in navigating the complexities of practitioner-based research. Enhanced mentorship focused on student experience and aspects such as time management etc would have been helpful. Whilst collaboration with peers is a strong point, I am not sure that there are many opportunities for professional networking with those in the profession, or within other educational organisations. This may emerge further down in the program.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Working with world experts on my topic, engagement with complex problems and accountability to do my best. There are not substantive changes I'd make. Better access to robotics platforms and computational resources as a part time student might be worth considering.

label: Complaint
keywords: engaged as a part time student
comment: Opportunity to have supported self directed leaning program. The University needs to better take account of the needs of mature, part time PhD Students with work commitments  outside the University. The communication /messaging is overwhelming and is geared to full time students where the university experience is (and should be) the totality of their daily activity. I want to feel engaged as a part time student, and resected for having other demands on my time, where I have made a commitment to make 20 hr per week available for my PhD study/work.

label: Complaint
comment: The program itself is fantastic. It is quite apparent that they focus on holistically teaching students to become well-rounded  practitioners. There is undoubtedly a lot of support from the faculty for all academic and extracurricular activities. Enrolment and class registration. It is a little confusing and I have missed deadlines several times because these enrolment deadlines and payment due dates seem to vary every year. This is especially problematic because I am an international student and there is often a lot of paperwork and due diligence that needs to be done in my home country before making payments to the university, considering these exceed $30,000 to $50,000 at a time. Frequent and differing deadlines tend to make this entire process quite complicated, and I still haven't been able to figure out how to make it easier.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Ability to gain insight from current leaders in the workforce, for example the head of cargo development in the new Western Sydney Airport. Allowing lecture recordings, because currently they do not provide lecture recordings as they say that people do not attend lectures if lecture recordings are provided but i feel that they could improve the attendance by making attendance taking compulsory so that those of us who need lecture recordings to review material are not inconvenienced.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: Patient instructor with friendly communication, online lessons and video playback features. More online activities so that students have better time allocation skills.

label: Complaint
comment: Engaging in discussions with other colleagues doing the same course providing and receiving feedback and explanations of the content. Honestly the three term system sometimes give pressure to lecturers and students to rush in teaching and learning the content, and it feels like a time after the term finishes it makes students forget little of what they learned during the course.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The best aspects include the wide array of resources available, such as CompleteAnatomy, cadavers, access to medical journals, online quizzes, extra revision material, etc. I also appreciated prerecorded lectures and in-person labs/tutorials which accommodated for my busy work schedule. I loved the lecturers who went out of their way and engaged their students with questions, jokes, and illustrations, as well as those that had neatly structured lectures that did not go beyond the scope of the learning outcomes. The quality of teaching in some departments needs to be improved, such as physiology. The broad nature of physiology does not justify making it so hard to learn - make lecture slides less crowded and more succinct. I also feel like there could be more revision resources available prior to midterms, such as practice exams. Tutorials could use more work as they sometimes can feel like forced groupwork.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: More practical experiences i.e. internships, field trips, placements etc.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: course could become a bit more social
comment: I have learned about how the economy works and I have also learnt how to manage my time with 2 degrees and work. Moreover, I have pushed myself out of my comfort zone and interacted with my peers in tutorials. Getting know some new people has been amazing. I believe the course could become a bit more social, so that people can interact with one and another. For example like doing group discussions and group tasks would be better.

label: Complaint
keywords: people at uni made it fun
comment: The people at uni made it fun and enjoyable. How cramped each of the assessments and assignments are. Maybe the trimester ain't the way to go to be honest.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment: The content and the opportunities. Getting the students to be engaged more.

label: Complaint
comment: Some lecturers are good. The Graduate Certificate in Information Technology program could benefit from improvements in several key areas. First, the recruitment standards for lecturers should be raised to ensure that candidates possess both strong English communication skills and prior teaching experience. This would help address the issue of lecturers who struggle with clear communication or lack basic respect for students. Additionally, lecturers who are clearly unqualified for the role should be promptly replaced to maintain the quality of education in the program.

label: Complaint
keywords: refused to give me a copy
comment: Some really passionate lecturers who have been very available to provide a lot of academic support when needed. Especially Melrose Brown. Outside of the compulsory subjects in some electives their has been some really unprofessional behavior from your lecturers. For one of my electives such as international law in global politics, no criteria sheet was uploaded for the assessments, instead we were just given general marking criteria sheets (which is actually against UNSW policy). I then randomly failed a major assignment which I was completely blindsided by (I have a distinction GPA) and when I asked for a copy of a marking criteria, the lecturer refused to give me a copy and took over a week to produce any response to my feedback (meaning I couldn't contest the grade, as appeals had to be processed within a week but you had to consult the lecturer first). To this day I've never seen a marked criteria sheet to this essay which I apparently failed (which is funny, I have an undergraduate degree in this field and have done several masters courses in this subject area and never failed an assignment before). This behavior is really unprofessional and sloppy from an academic institution. This is one example out of a few that makes me think I want to do my degrees through a more transparent university from now on. Whenever we raise these concerns with lecturers they just say "we are a research university we don't have time to upload course content and criteria sheets etc.". This doesn't fly with me, I did my undergraduate degree with ANU which is also a research university and they had plenty of time for professional conduct. It is a real shame because the lecturers of your compulsory subjects are highly professional and it is a shame that a small handful of your lecturers are ruining it for everyone.

label: Complaint
keywords: challenges to get consistent lab resources
comment: In 2024, I've taken two units of study (ZIET8042 & ZEIT8028) where there has been specific sessions through the semester with special planned industry related talks. These have both been excellent, and really puts the Master Coursework, study and theory into a practical industry context. For ZIET8042, [specified name removed] facilitated a special event and class talk with [specified name removed] [specified name removed] author of a widely recognised reference text in the field of Malware analysis. This was an incredible experience to interact and hear first hand experiences of a highly regarded industry professional. The ZEIT8028 facilitators [specified name removed] and [specified name removed] were regularly able to talk to industry related aspects of the course material, and [specified name removed] did a further session to focus on industry related aspects of the course material, applying what we have learned into real world situations, and thinking like a industry professional. I've also really enjoyed the most recent semester formats of intensive coursework in the first 6-8 weeks, then more focused assignment focus towards the end of the semester. Overall, has been a great learning experience. There has been some challenges to get consistent lab resources. The Cyber Security material is reliant on labs for learning outcomes and assessments, and it's been challenging with performance of the online labs, and consistency of operating local Virtual Machine. I appreciate there are no easy answers for this, and perhaps it should be an expectation for students in technical Masters Programs to have the skill to be able to manage this. Some of the more technical subjects would benefit from pre-requisite course material being suggested for students, so they are not learning foundational skills during semester. C++, Linux, Windows Command Prompt, Python. I have enjoyed the course content, but can see that learning these without the time pressures of assignments would have been a more enjoyable experience, and allow more focus on the security aspects of the coursework.

comment: It has helped me understand additional context for my job. The entire degree is essentially a cashflow exercise for UNSW Canberra. From my perspective, it appears that the university knows they will have a steady flow of students that the public service will fully fund, but does very little to make it worth the funding institution's (taxpayer funded) money. Lecturers recycle course notes from previous years, do little-to-no active teaching, and sometimes assign irrelevant text books that students must purchase (which in some cases were authored by the lecturer). I would be extremely disappointed if I was using my own money to pay for this degree, and am disappointed for the taxpayer. While some individual courses have been better than others, my overwhelming experience with this degree is that UNSW Canberra puts very little effort into actually crafting a worthwhile program of study, but happily accepts continued fees funded by the Australian taxpayer.

label: Harassment
keywords: lectures have no independent knowledge framework
comment:
This is the worst course and lecturer I've encountered in all my years at UNSW.
a. The course structure is chaotic. The lectures have no independent knowledge framework and are entirely read from the textbook. If that's the case, I might as well buy the book and read it at home.
b. The slides are very unclear, completely excerpted from the textbook. The instructor explained at the beginning of the semester that he took over the course on short notice, but it's hard to trust their competence and attitude when this remains the case for 10 weeks, even by the end of the semester. He even had time to travel, but no time to properly prepare the lectures.
c. The lectures and tutorials are almost entirely unrelated. Throughout the semester, there was no practice or reinforcement based on lecture content—only theory without application. The tutorials were entirely focused on PROC SQL-related exercises and did not reinforce the lecture material.
d. The class was disorderly, and the instructor had no control over the students. They only used attendance mechanisms to restrict students but did not offer engaging content to attract them.
e. The instructor lacks competence and communication skills. The assignment requirements were unreasonable, there was no reference grading rubric, and the feedback suggestions were not reasonable. Furthermore, when I raised reasonable follow-up questions, they could not provide a satisfactory answer or convince me.

label: Complaint
keywords: comprehensively improve your skills and attitude
I seriously doubt the instructor’s competence
comment:
Please comprehensively improve your skills and attitude as a teacher.
a. A reasonable course design is the foundation of the entire course. Lectures and tutorials should complement each other. Please refer to the design of the X course, which is a good combination of theory and application. The X course doesn’t need to check student attendance, yet the attendance rate is very high throughout the course because students don’t want to miss any class.
b. I seriously doubt the instructor’s competence. The instructor is supposed to guide us and be a role model. If you can’t do things well yourself, you have no right to teach us how to do them. The textbook can be a reference but should not be used to perfunctorily teach students. We need not only theory but also practical case studies. Putting the entire textbook on slides is not what a teacher should do. Taking over the course on short notice can be an excuse at the beginning, but it is not a justification for not preparing lessons for the entire semester. Additionally, an instructor who lacks competence should not be evaluating students. It's like asking an art teacher to assess my coding skills. For instance, the poor visual design of the lecture slides fails to convey information effectively. The slides heavily copy content from the textbook and lack the instructor’s understanding of the subject. Yet, when giving assignment requirements, you limit the presentation to only 4 pages, including one cover page, with no animations, and expect good visuals with multiple charts on one page that clearly show each metric. This is unreasonable, not taught in the course, and not mentioned in the assignment marking criteria.
c. Please align your career objectives as a teacher. As a teacher, you should be dedicated to imparting knowledge and helping us improve, not just assessing our level. Making things difficult for students and giving low grades is not your job goal; helping us learn is. Firstly, your brief is very poor—I don’t know the grading criteria for the assignments. There are only a few assessment items, such as word count and included information, but I don’t know which part is more important, like whether recommendations are 30% or analysis is 20%. There are no word count requirements, font size, or margins, only that the length should not exceed 10 pages. We have no reference points until the grades are released. Please refer to Professor X’s X and X courses, where she provides detailed guidance, helping us learn what each part of the assignment should include, and she gives detailed feedback and improvement suggestions when grading. Changing the assessment method after students submit their assignments is also not appropriate behavior.

Understand the need for supporting 'arguments' with evidence-based research, but didn't understand why such a focus was given to this component in the course. In corporate, there is often very minimal time to 'Acquire' and 'Appraise' and I felt that the focus placed on the four 'A's', particularly in Assessment 1 was excessive and not as relevant to the real world, at-least for me. Although I can appreciate the fundamental objective of this assignment was to challenge our 'teaming' ability, which it definitely did.
