comment:
The connection between the assessment and the content was unclear at times. The assessment is a very large amount of work to complete a project at work where not everyone has the required access to people or data to be able to compete it. 

The assessment had a lot about data and data analysis which was not in the course. 

Online forums were difficult to keep on top of and engaged with, the most helpful session was the one online videoconference to discuss that week's content but it wasn't assessed, an option to participate in one of those video conference discussions or do the online forumdiscussions or to alternate them would be better for people who have different learning styles.

All video conference sessions had limitied out of business hours availability and many people were expected to join during business hours which is difficult when you need to be employed to do the course and we all have day jobs to attend to, more out side of business hours sessions would have been helpful, especially when people organised their work day to attend a session and the course conveynor forgot and didn't show up to 2 of them and people had to try restructure their work time again. 

Too much course content to read, when being sold the certificate or X, the student admissions team boast about flexabilty and it only taking about 10hr a week commitment, in reality all the courses are 15 - 20 hours/week commitment and this one felt like 20 - 30 hours/week commitment to try absorb all of the information/readings and do such in-depth assignments.

Online courses seem to be based just on reading content which isn't helpful for people with different learning styles and neurodiverse people. If X can provide access to AI reading apps that can read the extensive content out in a natural voice that would be so helpful. Or if pre-recorded lectures could be an option, or more links to youtube videos and lectures that cover relevent content could be incorperated it would help to have a learning experience that isn't just a lot of reading which is very hard to fit into busy lifestyles or to engage with for 20-30 hours a week.

label: Complaint
keywords: issue with the way he is conducting
spoken at us for almost the entirety
comment:
I am in X's tutorial class. Along with many others, whom I have spoken to as a way to gauge my own reaction,  there is an issue with the way he is conducting the class. 
It's late in the day and I should have written earlier but here are a few examples of what has been occurring. 
- In the first week he laid out that if anyone had an issue throughout the term not to go to the course coordinator without going to him first. He also said to do this even if it was about him - as a sign of respect to him. At the time I found this odd but let it slide. However, it has contributed to why I haven't written earlier.
- Every tutorial he has spoken at us for almost the entirety of the two hours, about his life, about his connections, about his experience in business and how he can tell who is going to do well essentially by looking at them. (I'm exaggerating slightly). He also repeats these same stories each week. 
- At no point have we addressed the worksheets in a meaningful way. He would mention them but he would have no idea who has completed them or not. As this is an assessable component, I have no idea how this could be fairly adjudicated. 
- During assessment 2 he was not inside the room at all times, meaning some groups were presenting to no one for parts of their presentation. Mine included. How can this then be a fairly assessed component?
- I also don't appreciate him interrupting in every lecture almost as to one up the lecturer or the guest lecturer. Particularly in the week that X presented. For him to stand up and rally the "guys" after her lecture, felt ill judged. Even if his intention was good, it came across like he believed that what X said wouldn't have been heard unless supported by a senior male in the room aka himself.

label: Safe
keywords: passion for the subject matter shines through
comment:
X has a gift; his passion for the subject matter shines through, and is infectious. He has the ability to imbue otherwise dry content with the requisite enthusiasm to engage even the most burnt-out undergrad. 

This is in part due to his passion, in part due to his preparation, and in no small part his encyclopaedic understanding of the course – delivering insight with dry wit (which doubles as a good revision mnemonic). 

He is extraordinarily knowledgeable on the subject matter – enough to frequently offer relevant trivia (making for particularly handy dinner table conversational fodder). Demonstrated through his ability to explain complex and sophisticated international law concepts in clear, cogent terms – X makes tractable areas of law that have claimed the scalps of far more ambitious students in terms past. 

X clearly cares for his students, and this goes a long way in facilitating their engagement; with the tenderness of interacting with an oft-neglected shelter dog, X teaches with sufficient compassion to coax students into trusting that they won’t suffer at the cruel hands of public law once more. 

Whilst it can’t be measured through MyExperience, the strongest anecdotal testament to X’s teaching is the number of students who, prior to taking this course, considered international law ‘bullshit’; because of X, many are now considering a career in this field. From a jaded law student, this is one of the highest possible accolades.  

Furthermore, the delivery of this unit comes at a particularly interesting time; with the ongoing conflict(s) in the Middle East, the recent ICJ rulings, ICC warrants – there is much overlap between current events and course themes/content. As a Jewish student, there was much apprehension (both personally, and in the wider community) around taking this course in the current circumstances. X has navigated these circumstances with grace and class – he has facilitated important, meaningful, and relevant conversations around events/concepts with appropriate sensitivity. Most importantly, he has done so in a way that does not stymie discussion, nor preclude those from feeling like they can engage – irrespective of their political priors. This is in contrast to some feedback I have heard from students in other classes; it is a difficult feat to achieve, and X ought to be commended for it. 

X is an excellent educator; it is quite clear he has many opportunities available to him. The UNSW Law community is far richer for his decision to work here.

comment: I am writing a feedback regarding our instructor, X. I am extremely disappointed with the quality of her teaching. Firstly, I believe she is not proficient in business analysis and valuation, lacking both industry experience and necessary skills. During classes, she often needs to search for answers to many questions on the spot. Secondly, she is unfamiliar with the course structure, unable to provide immediate responses to any of my inquiries. Thirdly, I noticed that the teaching materials she uses differ from those of another instructor. The model materials that X emailed to us were completely absent during our classes. This course has been truly disappointing, and I haven't learned anything. The grading criteria for assignments are also very peculiar. I enrolled in this course because my friend recommended X, praising his extensive experience in valuation. However, when I attended the class, the teaching quality of this new professor was incredibly poor.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment:
Digital Strategy was the only course in my MBA that had a textbook, which felt regressive in that I didn't get the strong sense of 'the AGSM view' that I got from other subjects where the course notes were a synthesis of a wide range of sources presented in a relatively consistent format and tone.

The impact on study experience was trying to learn how to use VitalSource instead of downloading the PDF and highlighting notes in ways that can be easily referred back to me, and coming to rely on familiar behaviours of Adobe Reader. For me  the result was a lesser experience during the course.

The greater impact is not having course notes to refer back to when solving problems in the future, at my workplace, where I find the vast majority of the learning experience really occurs - and a stark difference to MBA graduates from other uni's who openly admit to not having basic executive management skills and nothing to refer back to (having git rid of their hard copy textbooks).

The subject matter in the textbook was useful to me as someone who works in technology environments but is not myself in a technical / technologist role. As a second-hand observation of those with more digital experience it seemed to broaden their horizons less than it did for me.

comment:
The structure. The video presentation was redundant. It had no connection to the rest of the learning. It also seems like a tax on the good students. For example if a student is constantly scoring near 100% in TAs, but now is losing marks because their team is not as interested. Especially in my group, it led to 2 people doing virtually all the work. most of the people in the group left the assessment learning nothing about the topic and had the scripts written by the group members who are striving for high marks. 

The TAs were a good way to stay up to date with the course content, especially in a way that's relevant to the course and the final exam.
Also the tutorials seemed pointless. The first 2-3 tutorials, I did the homework, as is the norm with the other course tutorials, but the tutorial spent the entire time solving the question line by line. Also we were given so much discussion time to solve the question as a group, but in all the tutorials I rarely heard any group even talking. Most of the time it was just 1-2 students who just filled out the excel sheet and participated. 

IMO a class participation grade is useful for the course. The tutorials should have 15-30 minutes of just asking the students for the answers for each line item, one by one. It encourages students to do the work at home. That 15 minutes should cover the doubts students have. The rest of the time should be new questions which the students should do in class under time pressure. This emulates the final exam environment and would be useful. It also would keep students who are up to date with readings stimulated during tutorials, as they were pretty dull even though we had a great tutor who was trying their best to keep it as interesting as they could.

label: Complaint
keywords: big waste of money and time
comment:
where do i start

1. My biggest issue with the course is that i felt like it's a big waste of money and time, and this is the general consensus across my group and my friends. 

Paraphrase what one of my group-mate said, "The course felt like ticking boxes, yes they've learned A, yes we have covered B, now do C." In other words, nothing is covered in depth, everything is on a more-than superficial level, such that it really does not make a lot of difference whether you know or not. For example, the lectures on business management, the "design" lectures, etc. I understand that the goal is so that the engineering students does not only know about technical stuff, but having a 2 hour lecture on "business management" and expect one student in a group of 6 to come up with some made-up stuff on the business side is not an effective way, for very obvious reasons.

Instead, i would rather you guys focus on 2-3 things, such as ethics, business management; and have everyone do the same exercise like what we did for the ethics report, so that people actually practice what's taught in the lecture, as otherwise we all know how little one would remember the concepts with no practices.

2. The group project -> We are learning stuff, but in a very inefficient way 

As engineering students, all of us wants to create impacts, but that driving force needs to be backed up with a strong belief of what we are doing actually makes a difference.

We only had 5 weeks for the project, so pretty sure the moment you started the project, you knew that this is just for the assignment, there is no way we could make an impact on a project-level scale in 5 weeks. Which is frustrating,  I would rather you guys do it in the extreme ways, such as

2.1 do it like what the electrical engineers are doing with their X course, have people actually create a working prototype and everything, focus on the actual product instead of bullshitting what it is suppose to do and "oh, we are gonna profit in 3 years based on this one number we found in this website"

2.2 do it properly like normal start-up business proposals, pay little emphasise on the technical side cuz of the little time we have, and much heavy emphasise on the business side, the delivery. mark based on how liable and workable the idea is, in terms of business operation, how easy is it for a non-engineering to grasp the idea, instead of what we did in the business plan, trying to get us to eat more than we can chew. quote what one of the course admin replied to "whether we need a conclusion for a business report", she said :"it's a business plan, but it's also a report. so you need to follow the format of a report". With all due respect, why does the conclusion matter when we are proposing an idea, like what are we concluding :"it's a good idea, you should invest 2 million cuz our mission is well-established?" what can't we conclude that we can't convey at the start?

the course has too much formal pretentiousness

label: Complaint

comment: Everything. The course lecturer needs to learn to organise the course or hand it over for someone else because they are extremely incompetent.  I acknowledge that there are always personal issues that hinder one's ability to teach or administrate. However, communicating the obstacles is part of professionalism. In this case, assessments were handed out WEEKS later than the intended schedule. This causes an overload on the second half of the term, which is when studying for the finals is the priority. We did not receive the first assessment until week 3. How is this even possible? We pay THOUSANDS of dollars and this is the result? If you cannot go through with your words, please hire more staff or communicate the issues that are ongoing instead of leaving all the students in the dark. Additionally, ELS students are heavily disadvantaged since no one is acknowledging responding to their needs. At first, I was sympathetic because there seems to be personal issues going on, but according to other students, the course lecturer has always been like this for other courses. So, this really begs the question as to why they are administrating the course. Stop saying 'assessments will be out in the next few days' when this is more of a joke than an actual statement.

label: Complaint
comment:
I've never felt the need to do a myExperience survey before but boy oh boy do I need to now.

This is without a doubt, the most disorganised,  repulsive, poorly planned, and demoralising course I have ever taken in my degree. It's amazing how despite constant feedback from students over the years does your faculty still manage to screw this course over, even with a new convenor who has supposedly "listened to feedback". Despite being unenjoyable in previous years, this year takes the cake of being the absolute worst and shows the course's extreme downfall since the departure of X. (This is my first time taking this course, but I have been in this degree long enough to know that only this studio has had ALWAYS had problems. I've been part of the X community in 2023 and have heard experiences from other cohorts).

First off, let us compare the rigidity of this studio compared to previous studios. In all previous studios, the course's project design/site has remained the same, with only slight changes to the actual briefs each year. This studio's project has changed so ridiculously much over the years, as your faculty tries desperately, with questionable effectiveness, to create a stable course without such a high fail rate (I know this is a fact). In 2021, the design project was to create a artists' studio. 2022, it was changed to the design of a library in Westmead (mind you, this was the year ArchSoc sent a petition from the cohort regarding the extreme poor layout of this course's assignments, of which the feedback has clearly been ignored). 2023, you change it AGAIN to the design of a library in Rosehill. This year, you go ahead and try to rework the briefs to the point it is somehow WORSE than the year before. What made you think it would be a good idea to shove both streams into one brief? You also changed the marking rubrics so it's worse, making it extremely specific (numerical) instead of the more generic rubric which made sense last year.

Secondly, the overall planning and organisation of this course has been without a doubt the worst in the entire degree. It is evident that the convenor has tried to rush to reworking this course and failed. Cramming together the 2 streams resulted in confusion and inaccuracies in the briefs, particularly in the earlier weeks. In A1, you clearly copied and pasted submission requirements from the previous year's briefs and not proofread it, resulting in regional requirements being mixed in urban. For example in A1c, the "work prepared in group" point was supposed to be in regional, but was actually not required. Also, the brief asked for 3 options, but in reality only 1 was required. Changes like these were revealed literally 1 week before their respective due dates. Absolutely no effort was made to edit the brief,  or even spend 30 SECONDS to announce on Moodle what was required to submit, leading to HUGE confusion and uncertainty in all tutorials

(CONTINUED IN NEXT SECTION)

label: Complaint
comment:
In assignment 2/3, the only thing that needed to be changed from last year was converting the required plans/sections from  the unusual scale of 1:250 to 1:200. That is literally all that needed to be changed. Instead, you throw in ADDITIONAL submission requirements such as a site plan AND extra drawings/annotations - who asked for you to do this????? I literally laughed out loud when you wrote an announcement on Moodle, saying how it is "disrespectful" of students to show up late and present at a time that "suits" them. I think it's even more "disrespectful" but also downright lazy how the convenor doesn't bother announcing ANY submission changes. For example, in EVERY OTHER studio, you are given 10 mins to present. In this studio, the most complex of them all, you go ahead and revoke it to FOUR minutes; only saying this THE WEEK BEFORE the due date. The brief clearly states 10 mins + 10 feedback for A1 and A2, but of course no effort was made to communicate changes on Moodle. Especially for the first design proposal (A2), giving students only 4 mins is malicious. And then today, our tutor emailed us saying we would be getting only 6 MINS for the FINAL, which is just cruel. Last year allowed for 10 mins in A2/3, why was it changed?! You slap on all these additional requirements for us to do, but then cut the present time by more than 50%. Repeatedly pulling 180s, changing the present time despite it stating 10 mins on the brief, ON EVERY ASSIGNMENT (4 TIMES) is irresponsible and unprofessional.

Third, the faculty has been lazy in marking of assignments, particularly in the first few weeks. In assignments 1b, 1c and 2, you force two tut groups into one. In 1a, you force the entire studio together. Why couldn't we just present within our own tut groups? That way, we can thoroughly learn about 4 or 5 precedents and REALLY UNDERSTAND it, than have 20 precedents shoved down our throats. You THINK it helps us "learn" but did you notice how the studio rooms became more and more empty as students left towards the EOD? Clearly, you are just trying to save time so you don't have to stay back as long for parity which I think is simply lazy.

The main issue stems from having a part time course convenor. It is NOT POSSIBLE to operate as a part-timer in the most important course of the architecture degree. It was originally supposed to be X, but was changed to X. WHY? Being part-time either means X is either not there to listen to feedback, or more likely, simply doesn't care. 

I just don't get how this course was STILL crafted so poorly after years of us students screaming similar things in your faces loud and clear. The fact that even after more than 3 years of the project design being repeatedly changed and course being desperately reworked to try to bring up the fail rate - shows that your faculty is either incompetent or has malicious intent - doing everything in its power to prevent students from passing. Embarrassing.

label: Harassment
comment:
What could be improved:
	• Several old articles that are a hard read, and lacked narrative.
	• The activities throughout the course do not relate to the assessment or discussion forums.
	• There is a lot of readings on general technology, less on models, concepts and theory.
	• Although I thought it was straightforward, there was confusion from students on the alternative posting for group A or B. This should be plain to understand from week 1.
	• Students did not realise they needed to post audio/visual content. Suggest using audio/visual content in your opening posts so students follow.
	• Applying full Harvard referencing style in posts kills the chat vibe. In my mind, the forums are a replacement for students talking in a breakout.
	• Assessment 2 is quite descriptive by nature. The questions refer to models that are not related to the course, e.g. Porters Five Forces is not in Unit 1-10, yet it is a question in the assessment (why?).
	• The questions in the forums don't lead to strong debate, again, they lead to a descriptive responses. e.g. describe scrum or CICD. This led to 20 posts starting with similar answers.

What did not go well:
	•  Most assessments allow students to deep dive into concepts. Assessment 4 does not allow students to interact with the lessons learnt from Units 1-10 effectively. More of an extension from the Marketing course in the MBA rather than a technology investigation.
	• Word limits were not enforced or respected in posts. In fairness, writing 200-500 words, some of it rambling, is significantly easier than attempting to write a succinct 100 or 200 word post.
	• Very detailed specific referencing requirements. In other courses, the lecturer has provided a simple one pager of what they expect for referencing. Problem solved. If the feedback to students is they should reference the UNSW Harvard referencing guide. Then I would suggest looking into the disparity of referencing syntax between Unit 6 and Unit 10. I went looking for a standard and could not find one.
	• I suspect Assessment 3 is susceptible to AI. Although the lecturers can only mark what is in front of them, it would be easy to spot outliers in the data, whereby students that received credit for all assessments then achieve 100% in their exam. I really like exams. However, this weakness should be pause for thought on its future applicability.
	• Reading 5.5 is long, industry specific and dated, there are many great alternative videos and articles on Blockchain. As is Reading 10.8.

comment:
I am a little disappointed in the last studio course in our degree. The reasons are below:


- The program  brief. This is the worst program brief I have ever seen throughout the six studios. The requirement is so unclear that the tutors don't even fully understand in middle of the term. As the program brief is so crucial to how you will approach  your design. This unclearness of the brief wasted us a lot of time figuring out how much space is really needed in some of the requirement.  

-The sudden change of presentation method in assignment 1 is absurd. And this sudden change is a decision made just before the presentation. This is really frustrating to the student as most of us have not prepare to public speak to so many peoples and most people cannot hear in the back of the Amphitheatre. This cannot help us learn  and is making the course looks poorly planned and lack of principle .

-The changing of the presentation time from 10 minutes to 4 minutes in one week before the presentation. Again, this really show the course is not welly planned and the lack of communication between the studio leader and tutors.

- The time management in the presentation day. I understand as there is limited time for the studio class and everyone needed to have the same amount of time for their presentation for fairness. But 4 minutes is really too short for a program that have multiple floors, explaining rich site context, the design approach and structure, etc. On the other hand, the feedback time is too long. 4 minutes presentation and 15 minutes for feedback time is ridiculous. I think the tutors should have a more good grasp of time in order for the presentation day to go smoothly.

- The double studio classes in week 7.  I am very lucky that my tutor offer a zoom video class in week 6 instead of having double studio class in Tuesday and Thursday in week 7. Having double studio class in between such a short period of time cannot yield a good learning outcome as design work takes time to develop. I would suggest move one of the studio class from week 7 to optional in week 6.

I really looking forward to design a library in the start of term. However, the overall experience of this course is quite chaotic and unprofessional.  I hope the course will change to be better for the next year third year students.

comment:
This course is truly the worst organized  course during my years in this university. 

- The moving between classrooms in tutorials. Why? 

-The content of the tutorial and the assessment are different in the lectures , for instance, the math formula,  This is truly unacceptable, this show the lack of communication between tutors and lecturer. And the unprepared unifying of  teaching material in between the teaching staffs. 

-lack of feedback and answer sheet. I will not understand what i have wronged in my assessment.

- the venue of the tutorials. If the tutorial need us to use a computer, why not choose the computer lab as the tutorial classroom instead of a normal classroom which have really less electric socket?

- Some part of the last assessment can be totally irrelevant to the content that taught in the lecture and tutorial. If you haven't taught some of the topic, please don't include them in the last assessment. It is just as simple as that. 

overall, This course is so poorly structured and lack the incentive to help us to learn. It is a complete downgrade from the previous course.

comment:
I think the thing I struggled with this course is what I have struggled with all the design studios which is a shame because they take up so much of this course. They are too interdisciplinary. If you're studying graphics, it's all right because the tutors you get tend to have some sort of graphics background and can give you helpful advice. But any disciplined outside of that you're sort of just left to figure your own shit out. I'm majoring in textile and object design and this is something I have struggled with throughout all the design studios. And it's a real shame because all the tutors you get are really qualified and educated but they're not always that helpful.

This is something I have continuously spoken about with my peers throughout this entire degree and it is really frustrating. This advice is probably too broad and changes with curriculums seems like a slog of a process but I just don't think the way this course is structured is all that efficient. And you spend 6 courses doing these 'design studios' or 'fada' courses whilst only getting 3 each for your actual majors. It just doesn't make sense and isn't practical.

I know this is probably brutal but I really feel like this has been a big let down throughout this entire course and it's really frustrating when your sending yourself into debt taking courses where you don't get all that much out of it. That being said, as I mentioned earlier, It was interesting to be able to explore one project in particular for this course. I just feel that there are a lot of improvements that could be made.

Could you combine the studios at least vaguely according to your disciplines whilst still having interdisciplinary collaboration? That way I think there would also be a stronger learning community around your different practices (which the degree is massively missing right now!!).

label: Harassment
comment:
The tutor X had created a very uncomfortable and damaging learning environment. My friends and I really enjoyed the content of the course and to give back to the course because of the great tutors we worked with at labs and help sessions, we decided to write course notes  to  support the learning of other students. These notes were created on Propagate.ink - my thesis project which aims to support content visualisation through visual hierarchy. We worked quite hard in translating our learnings into a unique visual form and we were proud of the time we took to make them. 

However, when we shared the notes, the tutor X unconstructively attacked the platform the notes were on, without even looking at the notes themselves. They chose to use discord and said "seeking vc funding vs seeking github visits" as they took it as an opportunity to drive students to their own notes on github by putting down our content. This should not be how tutors encourage learning. Then they said "#freeandopensourcededucation", "#nomoreaccounts", "#protest", "#studocu" about our notes which is incredibly discouraging, especially for it to come from a tutor who should be doing what they can to support others. Just because we had a sign up page before seeing the notes, Hae decided to make fun and attack our site in front of other students when all we were trying to do was create an inclusive learning space. I had a panic attack reading X's words on the discord as I couldn't understand how a tutor could be so rude to a student. X continued this by saying "unironically tho you dk how good this guys' engineering is so" to intentionally drive students away from our notes. Once again, this should not be what is acceptable for tutors who are core to a student's learning. 

In our forum post, X also said "free alternative" linking their notes, with many times on the Discord X implying that we are writing notes for external gain, not true. I dont understand why they had to use the word "alternative", they could say "Here are my notes too" creating a more inclusive learning space. With the context of the discord comments X made,  whether intentional or not, X's action of putting others down to elevate himself should not be what tutors stand for.

Only when X realised that they were conversing with one of the note contributors on Discord, did they begin to change their tone and stop the toxicity. Although tutors are also students, they should hold themselves to a professional standard.

I hope there can be more supportive tutors in future offerings. All students are aware of how smart tutors are, but how they can deliver impact is through positive interactions and contributing to an inclusive learning environment because this truly does make a difference and ultimately these are the interactions that a student remembers. I no longer find X a safe and inclusive learning environment if this is the response from tutors to students wanting to contribute.

label: Complaint
comment: X is just not a great teacher. He doesn't read the room or interact with students and just keeps reading off the slides in a monotone. The lectures were extremely difficult to focus on and actually learn anything from. If not for other explanations on youtube and online I would have failed this course. It is genuinely disappointing. I enjoy algorithms as a topic and learning about them. That's why I elected to do X which was a great course.

label: Complaint
comment:
The course administration needs to answer for it's short comings in helping X go through a personal emergency.
During week 2 X had an unfortunate personal emergency which caused everything to be delayed, rushed and outright difficult to learn/complete in adequate time
For instance, the X subshell wasn't working and I couldn't code haskell using the cse machines which was integral to the course. I had to use the ed playground to eventually solve assignment 1/ex04 which is unacceptable given the fees we pay to be here.
The computer science/engineering school needs to be more accountable for core subjects like this becuase if 500 students fail/struggle in a core class the problem isn't the students, it's the class.
If you want a solution have it so that there is a week 5/6 evaluation for every student like this so that the administration can get a feel on what's going on. Yes it may be too late but I'd rather have late help than a failing grade

label: Complaint
comment: Upon careful consideration, I have found this course to be the worst course I have done at UNSW. I have expanded more on the "what could be improved" section of this survey.

label: Complaint
keywords: not fit to be an educator
comment:
I think this course needs to be immediately overhauled. I have provided suggestions for improvements below: 

The first thing that could be improved is the immediate removal of X as the Course Convenor. He was extremely disrespectful, rude, unorganised and incompetent in being able to convene the course and needs immediate training on how to appropriately communicate with students and work in a learning environment. His management of the course and manner in which he has responded to students over course forums, had not been conducive to supportive learning environment. He is simply not fit to be an educator or work in any educational institute, and his conduct as the course convenor of this course has been abysmal and extremely disappointing. 

I also think the structure of this course needs to be overhauled. I, including many of the students I have spoken to, have found the case study structure to be an entirely ineffective way of teaching international law, given our assessments did not provide an effective or appropriate method of showing our knowledge given they were research based more than content based.  You cannot provide a couple of asynchronous "learning" classes on legal research and writing and then create a 40% weighted assignment that is so heavily weighted on legal research (it was a problem question, not an essay). It is extremely disappointing that this faculty/course, and more broadly, this law school, as a means to make more money or save on resources and effort, has resorted to an "asynchronous" learning structure in lieu of putting in more effort to craft more considerate course structures and assignments.

label: Complaint
comment:
This course is an unmitigated disaster. By no small margin, this has been the worst experience I have had in a COMP course, or indeed, in any course. It is a shame this means the course is likely to be shelved due to this, even though the content itself is interesting and has been a well-loved course in all offerings prior and issues are entirely due to execution, rather than anything inherent in the content.

Personal tragedy is unfortunate and unpredictable, but the staff collectively has let the course progression slip and have diligently been unable to recover at every opportunity to do so. Further, outside the LIC, it seems the course admin was also busy with their personal academic pursuits abroad.

On the part of the school, there should have been processes in place to lift some burden where a staff member, or multiple, are unable to continue with the course - the highest levels of faculty were made aware of the situation early in the term.

The disruption of this course is not short of that seen in the initial COVID outbreak and I strongly believe SY grading should be given to students as an option.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: accent was a bit hard to listen to
comment: Sometimes the accent was a bit hard to listen to. A specific thing for me was enunciating the "y" sounds. You pronounced year and ear the exact same which is distracting and confusing in the context of Cochlear.

label: Complaint
comment:
Her English skills were very low, she struggled to pronounce words. Her articulation was difficult to discern. Her projection was low so that it was difficult to hear, and she spoke in a monotone voice so that it was hard to engage. She read off her computer the entire class. She rarely seemed positive or actively interested in the students around us. Did not know any one's names, or interested in class participation. Very difficult to understand, no point in attending class because we did not learn anything. 

Class could be improved by engaging more with the class (not reading from computer) and projecting voice and speaking with passion/ enthusiasm/ tone.

label: Complaint
comment:
If assignment briefs were done correctly in the first place and moodle site was better organised weekly... similar to studio 4 and 5, students would understand the benchmark of what is required weekly. I think that because tutors and convenors assume that they understand students and are making it easier for us but you guys really are not. I agree that because we are in our final year now we SHOULD in theory know what is expected... but similar to students staff teaching styles differ, some expect less, whilst others expect more but most importantly, none actually tell us what they want. So if we were given a generalised guideline, it would give us a stronger foundation to work towards. We already have SO much on our plates individually, each of us have varrying experiences, struggles etc. So please make it easier for us to stay organised. It's as simple as putting something like "week 2: have sections, plans etc. Ready for review". 
Furthermore, the amount of emails and notifications we recieve weekly before the course even started was UNBEARABLE, frankly it's alot to read through especially because they're just changes made to the brief in conjunction with the theory that actually matters, it's genuinely confusing and extremely overwhelming. 
Additionally, having classes twice a week every two weeks would be helpful in theory... if the classes weren't literally a day apart 3 days before submission.
With so little time to actually work on anything and so much information to stay on top of, criticism could be a lot less harsh and rude, not just personally, but others too had agreed that Rachael could be quite harsh when it comes to critiques. 

Most importantly! Please don't forget that you guys are supposed to be on our side!

label: Complaint
comment:
The group task was not well designed and was very very demanding. Given that the content required for the task was only taught in week 8 having 2 weeks to produce something of value is not possible. Moreover, the amount of deliverables for the task was impossible to complete all to a high standard. The task should be a video OR report not AND. The dataset for the task was also too big and required hours of training. 

Moreover, the need to use GPUs for training is absolute bullshit and should have been highlighted earlier. The project basically wanted us to create a full research paper on the Wildscenes dataset which is not possible to do given we have 3/4 weeks for it when training a model take 2 hours. 

All in all the design of the group project was very very poor and I will be taking it up further.

label: Harassment
comment: Providing practical and useful feedback, he often focuses on little insignificant and irrelevant points, that doesn't help the project as a whole. His method of teaching needs a lot more improvement, making students do compulsory extra work that other classes don't do, threatening the students with grade marks which is highly unprofessional and unreasonable! As a tutor, his design ideas are quite fixed and pushes students in the direction he wants them to go with his own design logic, resulting in half the class showing up with extremely similar plans and ideas. He needs to be more open to individual ideas, and provide support to let students achieve their own design project. Also his time management needs further work, every week going 1/2-1.5 hrs over lessons ending times, expecting students to stay for his feedback which isn't helpful at all. His expectations of students putting in considerably large amounts of money and time every week to show their work is also unfair and too much. He said we should work several hours every day for this project, yet we have other courses to complete, whilst also balancing personal time and work so that we can afford living expenses and extra costs to put into our project. He has an idealistic impractical view and expectation, which we can't achieve. I think it's good for him to gain more working practical experience before teaching to be very honest.

label: Constructive Feedback
comment:
I was hoping for a more critical perspective on the history that is taught in final year high school; for the most part it felt like a recap of everything I learned in modern history and I am fairly sure most students in the class had also completed history in their schooling. Personally for me it was a bit of a wasted subject which is a shame. To reiterate, improvements could be made through more critical perspectives discussing how gender, race and class significantly divided the experiences of people in the 20th century instead of treating countries as monoliths that all had the same experiences. 

I also believe that using these critical perspectives along with explaining how these ideas are still relevant to the modern era would add to a more comprehensive understanding of the history. It would make it obvious why we learn the events that we do and what that reflects about the current ideological landscape.

During the iLab when myself and peers asked questions about the materials (during periods where he stated he'd accept questions), he would tell us to stop doing anything he hasn't explicitly told us to do and to stop thinking beyond his instructions. label: Complaint
keywords: acted as though I was stupid
comment: Found him to be rude, blunt, and not provide any meaningful help. Whenever I would ask him for help, he seemed as though he could not be bothered to answer, and acted as though I was stupid for even asking. Whether he intentionally meant this or not, I am not sure. I have multiple friends in this course who share the same opinion, so it is not just me. who has this perception. We all would have found this class much more enjoyable if my friends and I felt like we could ask questions without being made out like idiots for doing so.

label: Harassment
keywords: lack any useful or meaningful content
comment:
This, in all seriousness, may be the most disorganised and maladministered course I have had the displeasure of encountering in my experience.
- The course content is extremely poorly delivered by X. She rambles on all class in a semi-incoherent manner, with no order or structure to the content. It is merely reading off the slides that in itself lack any useful or meaningful content, intermixed with her random tangents. The content jumps from point to point with no logical order, which makes it very hard to understand what is going on. Not to mention, when X suddenly left for a few weeks, there was ZERO communication between the substituting lecturers. Each one would simply come and ramble about the same content which the lecturer covered in slightly different terms. The readings are long and arduous, and are multiple 20+ page articles, which is simply not feasible to handle amongst other tasks and life duties.
- The assessments are beyond ridiculous. Barely any, if at all, link to the course content taught. The midterm was completed almost entirely independent of the course content - which makes you wonder what the point of the course is; is it not redundant if we conduct our learning ourselves? Not to mention, the fact that they gave only 10 days to complete a 1800 word assignment is unfair enough, but the lack of sheer competence to even proof read the assignment is unbelievable. Changing the assignment 3 days into a 10 day assignment and then refusing to listen to student complaint re. an extension is crazy  - why don't you try do this assignment in the time constraints, X? Your lack of empathy never fails to amaze me. Not even that, but to turn around and make the final exam 3500 words in 10 days is even more inane.

X should be ashamed at the state of the course he is running. Of course, given his conduct in refusing to listen to emails (not even, he showed a disgusting attitude where he takes the view that he and he alone is right) which were sent on behalf of the student body, he is clearly indifferent to complaint and feedback, so I fear my report will go unnoticed. The way he looks down upon valid complaint is truly like nothing I have ever seen before. I would hope the Dean looks into this matter and seriously considers X's dismissal, as it very clearly and in no uncertain terms demonstrates X's incompetence as a course convenor and his arrogance prevents him from implementing much-needed change. No student should have to experience the stress, ununiformity and general chaos of this course.

label: Complaint
comment: Nothing.  Out of all the lecturer's I have had in University I would say X is the worst.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: content needs to be split up more
comment: I think that the content needs to be split up more. The amount of content that is taught in one week can be quite a lot and in reality you are teaching students to cram in a lot more information into short term memory, which will then be forgotten about in the long term. I think it's worth teaching foundational content, and then students that major in anatomy or have courses that really need to learn about the whole body, can take a part B course. The fail rate for our exams are a lot higher than what you would expect from a well taught course. Cadaver images should also be used in the lecture, so we learn how to identify vessels well from a 2-D perspective. We get 5 minutes to see how to identify one specific region in the body as the tutor comes around, and then we move on for the rest of the class. We don't ever get to see that part of the body again for the rest of the term, and there are no images of it on the lecture to refer to, this contributes to a lot of lost marks in the SPOT tests.

label: Complaint
comment:
i believe he did not fully understand the questions that my team asked during a qna in the week 9 tutorial, additionally cutting us off before we finished our statements. Criteria and notes within group assignment briefs seem too strict, for example, video audio that seems sped up risks not being marked, process diagrams displayed landscape risk not being marked, going even one work above word count not allowed. 

The week X filled in for X was the most disengaging tutorial I have ever been a part of. Voice was not loud enough, most monotonous voice, and sentences were not pronounced properly, making it hard for students to understand.

Terrible structure of course. Having half of a group project due 3 weeks earlier is unheard of not very beneficial. RAT quizzes should be due right before the student's tutorial class, like other courses that require pre-tutorial work being done. Students like myself and others work full time, and needing to complete the RAT at an ambiguous time is not beneficial. 

Group projects in which most groups have 4 members while my team only had 3 is clearly unfair. every group should be equal, even if it meant bringing students from different classes together. Or, the groups that have less members will be required to complete slightly less of the workload. In my case, my group had 3 members, where one member has been completely absent from project activities or is doing their part completely wrong, leading to another team member and myself completing everything. Having only us 2 doing the workload of 4 people is incredibly harsh.

Recorded lectures have its benefits but from someone having no initial knowledge and with the course being a Gen ed, in my experience learning in person and being able to ask questions makes learning much easier and engaging.

label: Other
comment: nothing. rude.

label: Safe
comment:
I found the lectures to be the highlight of the course. I especially appreciated one of the earlier lectures by X's coworker, who demonstrated how to use cloud point data to generate models and analyses of sites. This topic could have been explored more in-depth and possibly integrated into the course. I was unable to implement what was shown on that day which I regret. 

X's lecture on Sydney's development was provocative and powerful -  political and cultural insights should be more present in the course. As this was our first brief on a public architecture typology - the coal loader being a wider site-driven project - I think it is important that we are informed of more experiences like this. It was insightful to hear the struggles an architect faces, even the most successful ones. I personally often question the agency I will have if I choose to pursue this as a career, so I appreciated the rage X expressed.

The reading list was also comprehensive and I appreciated the breadth it covered - I wish it could have been integrated into studio teachings.

label: Complaint
comment: The content is way too heavily concentrated on the theoretical concepts that contextualise Art. Often the lectures would be ten minutes away from finishing and then the lecturer would remember to talk about actual artworks, as if it were an afterthought. Even then, the lecture would finish without having gone through all the art examples. The lecturer would then dismissively say that we can catch up on this in the tutorial, which often didn't happen, as the tutorials also treated the artwork examples as an afterthought as well. This is Art, not Sociology. The lecturer leaned way too much on these concepts. and both my lecturer and tutorial teacher were both unapproachable. Their email responses were short and unhelpful. My primary concern was with my tutorial teacher, X, who had been rude and dismissive of me in tutorial classes, to the point where I had to exit class multiple times to settle my emotions. Most of my questions were met with retorts and sly remarks, especially if I ever 'preempted' an upcoming slide on her presentation (X's words).  X didn't like when the class discussion slid slightly out of her own schedule. It made for a very controlling, frustrating class where I and my peers felt as if we couldn't ask questions for fear of being ridiculed and shamed (passive-aggresively) by X. There was no room for error with X, she would interrupt whenever she felt it would demonstrate her authority, not giving the space to the student to justify their ideas or even finish their point. I'm a very participatory student, and I felt silenced, like I could never get anything right, even though I did my due diligence and studied. There was no room for productive debate and discussion across the class, even during our group discussion assessments. I didn't even get through my first point in my group assessment without her interrupting me to talk about something else instead, not considering that I might have been leading to it. In general, I felt targeted, my participation was never celebrated but  stomped down on. By week 8 many students had not attended the tutorials, and students hesitated to speak up when prompted. I can sense I'm not the only one disliking the course. X needs to do better and the course content needs to balance art with the sociology side. I felt like I wasn't doing a Fine Arts class, I just wanted to leave.

label: Complaint
comment:
Please refer to my 'what could be improved' response.
TLDR; X is a controlling and officious teacher who needs to drastically upheave her teaching style. I felt ridiculed, targeted, and silenced by her.

label: Harassment
comment:
-Not rolling his eyes when students ask questions.
-Not saying 'obviously' under his breathe after answering questions. Clearly it isn't that obvious if we are asking questions in the first place.
-Not breaking 1hr lectures up into 2-3 parts that add up to 2-3hrs.
-Not randomly leaving the lab intermittently. 
-Not using other lecturers slides.
-Showing any interest whatsoever in teaching the class.
-Understanding that just because he has decades of experience in this field doesn't mean his students do.
When told that the sound on a lecture wasn't working, he said that he was 'very busy. I might get around to fixing it if I have time.' Disappointing response considering how much we are paying for the course.

Overall X is transparently disinterested in teaching and makes students feel dumb for asking questions in an attempt to try and learn. Quite possibly the most disheartening teaching I've ever experienced - no one should be made to feel stupid for asking questions.

label: Complaint
comment: The overall management and organisation of the course was extremely poor. Quiz release dates were unscheduled and due at different times every week. Assignment notification had a few hiccups when released and had to be revised, as well as the due date being extremely unclear. Additionally, the assignment itself was extremely vague and the criteria was not very defined. When questions regarding the assignment were asked on the forum, the replies would either be extremely vague or even inconsistent with replies on other posts. Another concern was about the word limit of the report. The assignment brief had said 1k words max, however when asked for leniency on this word count Anthony confirmed that 1.2k was fine without providing an announcement on this matter which could leave some students out of the loop regarding this.

label: Harassment

label: Complaint

So, where do i begin?
X really does not know how to deliver academic content to students. The structure in his slides and lectures, or should i say the lack of thereof, always leaves more questions than answers. He also spends too much time telling us his experience in X which, respectively, we dont care much about. We care more about our grades and what we learn in class. But guess how much time he spends on the syllabus, 10 seconds. Everytime he says 'this is your course syllabus' and moves on to something else. 

Students would appreciate having a guest lecture occasionally to hear about experiences, but having 'guest' lectures throughout the term is ridiculous.

Overall I cant refrain from expressing my disappointment towards the teaching quality of X.  Without a doubt, the most incompetent lecturer i ever had in UNSW, and I believe it would be better off for all future X students if X could make the decision to go enjoy his post-retirement life :)

label: Complaint
comment:
Overall, the site and brief are equally lack of thinking for a 12UOC graduate studio.

The brief greatly restricts the students' design opportunities with an excessive number of overly detailed space requirements. I seriously question the value of these specified numbers of tables and chairs as positive guides for designers. Instead, broader spatial areas and functional divisions are needed. Requirements like the number of chairs and tables should not be a predetermined parameter, and its prominence in the designer's initial considerations is extremely absurd.

The site is incompatible with the object to be designed.

Firstly, the site is neither diverse nor strong in a single natural or urban quality. The connection to its northern hydrological elements, the parramatta river, is disrupted by the strict boundary demarcation, and the transformative attribute of Rosehill neighbourhood was neither highlighted nor developed in the site analysis and precedent study, thus flattened in further stages of design.

Secondly, the design requirements are impractical. The proposal for a large multi-purpose complex on an relatively small site creates an untenable conflict between the required space and the actual size. To make it worse, the brief imposes additional restrictions on vertical building height despite the already constrained horizontal site area, compounding the design issues. These constraints seem illogical and counterproductive, undermining the feasibility and effectiveness of the design.

There is also a major issue with the selection of the tutor team. Not all tutors possess the necessary knowledge base, open-mindedness, and tutoring ability to provide constructive advice, which can be quite disappointing.

label: Harassment
comment:
X is overall incompetent, he lacks knowledge and spends most of his time in class referring us to other staff who actually know what they are doing. 

He held me back more than he benefited me through this class. I only began to find my footing after reaching out to other staff to do the job he should be doing. 

He has a poor attitude, he goes on his phone or stares out the window when students are talking to him. 

He falsely accused me of not attending class (I missed one due to COVID). He failed to give me feedback, then when I mentioned this to him he exploded in a rage, he loudly yelled "your work is fucking garbage" which the whole class heard, that is a word for word quote. Having my assessments marked by the same man who cant control his emotions is a joke and i am disappointment in the university. 

You would have to do a good job to convince me my mark was not influenced by his personal opinion of me. The reason I am so harsh on him is because my future depends on this, having an incompetent egomaniac as a tutor is terrible.

Part of this assessment was process work, X actually took a pencil and drew over my sketches.

He severely  lacks technical knowledge, every time a student asks a question about working with a material he tells us to research it ourselves or talk to X in the lab. I don't believe he actually know the information, so what purpose does he serve? I really would like to hear why he is qualified to teach, it astounds me how little he knows.

Working in his class becomes a game of satisfying HIM, instead of focusing on making a good product, you are forced to play a game of satisfying X's wants. We have to present information to him in a different way to all the other staff because he doesn't have technical knowledge. We genuinely have to dumb down our work and process for him. 

Part of this "dumbing down" is due to his lack of knowledge with CAD/CAM software, he referred to my high quality renders as "smoke and mirrors". He does not see CAD as a legitimate part of design process, He told me its a waste of time, devoting hours to model something with cad when it could quickly be made from foam or card. 

I have 6 years experience using multiple CAD/CAM software, it is so much faster for me to CAD and simulate models than make them from paper. I see the benefit of physical modeling, they are especially valuable if a product is hand held or has touch points. However his refusal to accept that CAD has a place in process is ludicrous, again we have to do our assessments FOR X not with him.

He at minimum needs a teaching assistant with technical knowledge.

He sees his method of process as the only way, he crushes creativity and innovation. 

He is the largest reason I am questioning partaking in the honors program.

label: Safe
comment:
This has been my favourite course at uni so far!

- structure: having many lectures at the beginning of the term and then stopping makes sense. The change in pace allows me to stay engaged with the course, and the guest lecturers would leave me excited about not only the course but architecture in general! As someone who isn't entirely sure what I want to do with my career, seeing all these passionate people is invigorating. 

- X: when the presentations for the Urban and Regional stream were done last term, X's obvious passion and love for cities made it very clear that the right choice for me was the Urban stream.  Thank you for transmitting your passion, for inspiring us to go above and beyond our understanding of architecture (I enjoyed the politics rants), and for stopping to review student's designs. Your feedback helped me understand my own design and express ideas that I'm not yet capable of explaining architecturally.

Thanks X and X for an amazing term!

label: Safe
comment:
- incentivizing us to go to the lectures: I wouldn't have gone to as many without his emphasis on attendance, and I learnt a lot from them. Knowing that he'd be there at the lectures (even though he's already listened to them for years)  helped keep us in check. 
- his passion for architecture!! By *far* the most engaging tutor. He tries his best to get us excited about our degree, which should be our responsibility, but in any case it makes a huge difference to my personal motivation to do the coursework and get involved in extracurricular (AIA) events. 
- even during presentations where I think 'wow this is a boring scheme', X finds something the student has done to make their work stand out, and makes it a learning experience for the class. He tries to bring out the architect in all of us. His teaching style (and X's) has helped me understand that half the job of teaching is understanding what the student is trying to say, and help us understand it as well. I'm always amazed at how he continues to actually think throughout the whole day of studio or a whole day of presentations. 
- he never makes students feel bad or stupid for our mistakes! Or for not doing enough work for the week.
- he gives examples of great/not great architecture around us. Lots of times tutors say 'look for inspiration around you', and it's hard to know what to look for without a trained eye. X teaches us what to look for.
- this is a big one: he doesn't discount an international student's work because of a language barrier that may make it difficult for them to explain their work. This ties in with the 'understanding what the student is trying to say'. I haven't seen any tutor put as much effort into helping international students the way he does. As an international student, this makes me feel safe and welcomed!
- overall his patience and cheerfulness are also a highlight. 
- always stays after class to make sure everyone has had a good session of feedback. 
- available for emails

Thanks X for an amazing term!

label: Complaint
comment:
Being kinder to students - I think a lot of people felt attacked throughout the term. He was sometimes quite rude to students and it was unnecessary and borderline inappropriate in a learning environment. 

Class participation - He usually called on people instead of picking from a show of hands in response to a question. This made it really difficult to get CP, and even more so for the international students because he never called on them. It was mostly X who was the only white male in the class other than X himself. I was called on sometimes too. 

Mid-term feedback - The feedback was aggressive. I was going to have my essay reviewed but ultimately missed the deadline. His feedback really conveyed to me that he doesn't know what he's doing. For example, he made a comment that my opening sentence in my intro was broad when the UNSW essay guidelines recommend and broad introductory sentence in an essay. He called my argument an analogy and I think a more experienced teacher would have recognised it as an argument. He also told us to use the X case study in the essay and then critiqued me for using it in the essay. 

Textbook - More than once, he mentioned that he didn't look at the textbook. How am I to trust anything my teacher says when he hasn't even put in the bare minimum effort to read the textbook? How do you teach a course well without reviewing the readings that your students have done? Unprofessional and lazy. 

Pass/fail - In literally every single class, he liked to remind us that a massive percentage of students fail. Even after a student in our class asked him to stop talking about it because it causes a lot of anxiety, he continued to mention it. He also told us a week before the essay marks were released that 40% of our class failed the mid-term which is just an appalling lack of tact and common sense. He caused so much stress and anxiety by repeatedly mentioning it. And what's worse is that he seemed to derive some perverse amount of joy from that percentage. He always spoke about as if he was bragging about how many students he failed. I did X in Term 1 this year and not once did X, who has taught this subject for many years, even mention the pass/fail rates in the course. She just wanted us to pass and that's how it should be. I don't think X wants us to pass. 

Overall, I think that X genuinely thinks he's doing the right thing and he just doesn't recognise that sometimes honesty is not the best policy and sometimes the best thing to do is to keep one's mouth shut. I hope this feedback helps the faculty to reflect on what type and calibre of teacher you want in this notoriously difficult subject.

label: Complaint
keywords: makes a mockery of my time
comment:
The textbook and Overall course structure.

I understand that the purpose of the course was to incorporate as much policy/critque as possible to encourage students to think deeply about whether the law should be the way it is, but for a course like this - which can make or break a graduate seeking to practice law - I think there should be a much stronger focus on the substantive law as it is.

I quite like the theory/critique components of the course, but I think the way the textbook is written makes it incomrehensibly dense and difficult. For e.g. the discussions on X was painful to sit through and the theory/critique just blended in with the 'substantive' parts of the readings. I would have liked to see a stronger and clearer separation to the effect of "Here's the law - and now here's why it's shit" so that engagement with the theory is made more meanigful. Basically, I think you should be given a firm grasp of what the law is so that it's easier for you to spot the cracks and appreciate the deficiencies.

More on the textbook:
- It makes a mockery of my time.  For e.g. there was a substantial portion of the Judicial warning chapter dedicated to what the Roman Law on 'proofs' was. I get why the topic was explored but the matter should have been highlighted by way of a well-placed footnote or two and a link to further reading for those that are interested and have the time for it.
- The textbook also runs around and between concepts like a headless chicken. Sure, evidence can be relevant for more than one purpose but I don't think the matter should have been taught through the lens of hearsay examples. Something like relevance is meant to be the simpler part of evidence law and also its core foundation - keep it as simple as it can be. [See also readings on proof - why was the Prasad direction and its abandonment discussed before the student had a chance to fully appreciate what BARD actually entails? How is the student meant to understand why the evidence wasn't capable of meeting the Crown's standard of proof when they hadn't yet read about what that standard of proof even is?]
- The structure of the textbook and course - I understand why the course decided to teach Pt 3.11 right after Pt 3.1 but why jump to Credibility right after? And WHY cover Character before tendency, coincedence and crucially, Opinion? s 111 was entirely useless to me until i later covered expert opinion readings. Similarly the readings for Character dintinguishing tendency and context evidence was unbelievably frustrating since I hadn't covered those concepts properly. There's a reason why the ALRC and NSWLRC organised the Act in the way they did after spending a decade or so on it. Please don't dilly dally around with it when there seems to be no good reason for it (unlike with teaching Pts 3.1 and 3.11 first)

This is all i can type so I'll leave it there. Sorry for grammar/spelling errors.

comment:
His feedback and advice throughout the term has been completely useless. His marking throughout the term has been completely unjustified. He does not use the rubric whatsoever, and instead just comes up with numbers based on what he feels like. He even explicitly told students that instead of marking by the criteria, he marks "too an average" - ie. before marking, he chooses an average he wants the results to be, and then awards students marks on either side of that so they add up to the average. This is not at all a valid method of marking, and has completely screwed over students in his class.

He also marks students down for matters of opinion (eg. i think it would have been cool if your design had this feature as well, and so bc you didn't include that I'm deducting marks) which once again has nothing to do with the criteria or the quality of content, and everything to do with his own opinion. Furthermore, he will deduct high amounts of marks for things that shouldn't be penalised (eg. a group member in your group stuttered on a sentence whne presenting, so im deducting 4% off your overall mark).  The only thing he's looking for from us is for us to reproduce EXACTLY what he did when he took the course, and anything even slightly different will result in a bad mark.

However, the biggest issue is that his marking is completely unjustified - he will choose a random number seemingly based off vibes, give a few vague comments based on his opinions, and not explain how or why you received any of your marks, nor refer to the criteria whatsoever (which I don't think he realises should be used to mark). For example, for the ethics report, the criteria for receiving 4/10 marks for the introduction is: "few ethical issues identified and described, but the context and stakeholders are not identified". This would mean that discussing stakeholders, ethical issues and context AT ALL in your introduction should result in higher than a 4/10, however X would give students 4/10 when they clearly had all three things, because he didn't like the way it was written or something. If the criteria isn't being used to mark it, what is the point of having it there in the first place??

He has made this subject unnecessarily stressful. I was not expecting X to be my lowest course mark across my whole uni degree, especially after putting decent effort into it throughout the term.

Face the class when you're talking to us! A lot of the time you were facing the whiteboard so it was hard to understand. 

Write with big letters. Your writing was really small which made it very difficult to read and copy down.  Even if sitting in the front row it was still a struggle to read everything you've written. 

Don't just copy down the solutions. Provide some examples or a basic overview of the general approach to solving these problems. Try asking X for some tips as he subbed in for you when you were away, and it seemed like a lot of the class reacted positively and were engaged with his teaching style.

label: Complaint
keywords: decision making in this course was suboptimal
comment:
TL:DR: Course-directing, especially the management and decision making. 

X did a frankly terrible job in directing this course which could've been a great learning experience otherwise. The first (assessable) exercise was released in Week 4 where many students including myself struggled to apply our knowledge as were finally touching the language halfway into the course! Moreover, the  decision making in this course was suboptimal at best. When it eventually came to "prioritizing the wellbeing of students", it was DEFINITELY not in our best interest to simultaneously release an assignment and exercise together in the penultimate week of the term. To be fair, the release of assignment 1 was okay since we were provided with adequate time but releasing an assignment + an exercise pushing week 9 seems completely unjustified and absolutely does not satisfy the student wellbeing motive.  As everyone within this course is aware, an average student cannot be expected to fish out their tiny knowledge they've gained from this course and put it into an essentially full-scale Haskell project disguised as an second assignment (unless you've done an ridiculous amount of self-learning which at the point, why are you doing this course!).  It also doesn't make sense to me how an assignment takes so long to be pushed out even though its the exact same as the one made for last years running, My condolences to all the staff that clearly have a passion for this work, I hope this course gets shed in a better light in the future.

comment: I think it would be better to have 2 separate moodle 'rooms' for Urban and Regional as often times there was miscommunication, confusion and accidental overlaps e.g. in the urban stream brief there was requirements to study the site in parramatta in relation to Kempsey contexts, an obvious mistake that can be misleading if not rectified. Further, the amount of assignments due I believe was a bit excessive. Within the first 4 weeks students were asked to submit precedent studies AND architectural strategies which although is quite common for studio, by splitting them into 2 separate hand in's, this created more work and for many students they simply chose not to submit a precedent or one that was not satisfactory due to the low standing of the weight marking i.e. 5%. Overall, these assignments could have been condensed so as not to overwhelm us within the first few weeks. However, this was better spread in the later weeks with the final 2 assignments.

label: Constructive Feedback
keywords: Quiz information and questions could be asked more clearly
comment:
Quiz information and questions could be asked more clearly regarding what you want us to answer and please provide us with sufficient information to answer the question. 

Please be more transparent with the mark allocations in terms of whether or not partial marks will be given and how these marks have been calculated. 

Please provide specific sample solutions to each specific quiz question instead of general description of questions and general solutions. Reasons:
-  We spend a long time trying to match the general description of questions with their specific quiz question (even with Ctr F) as the description of the question is sometimes vague. This is particularly prominent with question descriptions that ask for lots of journal entries. Without showing us the original questions with numbers, it is incredibly difficult for us to figure out how the numbers in the journal entries have arisen. The explanations next to the general solutions assist our understand but by no means does it replace the background information/ context that we would be given if the actual question to the general solutions is given. 
- Even though you expect us to learn the thought process and apply it to our specific question, without providing specific answers to our personalised questions, we cannot know if what we've written is correct. To rectify this situation, I had to attend multiple consultations to get my answers checked. It would be more efficient if we were directly given the answers to our personalised quiz questions. 

When teaching the lecture and in any video recordings, please elaborate more from the slide particularly showing us where all the numbers come from and the reasoning behind such working out rather than giving us a reading of the slide.
